source_version: IUPAC Blue Book 2013 online version plus post-V3 web corrections

records.title: INTRODUCTION
then: select_and_name_parent_structure_before adding prefixes infixes or suffixes
allow_general_iupac_names only when unambiguous and recommendation-conformant
records.source_quote: For nomenclature purposes, a structure containing at least one carbon atom is considered to be an organic compound and can be named using the principles of organic nomenclature, such as substitutive or replacement nomenclature, as described in this book.

records.title: SCOPE OF NOMENCLATURE FOR ORGANIC COMPOUNDS
then: include carbon compounds and specified Group 13-17 hydrides in organic nomenclature scope
route excluded domains to their governing nomenclature systems
tables_or_terms: Table 1.1 Elements included in these recommendations
records.source_quote: For nomenclature purposes we consider all compounds containing carbon as the principal element to be organic compounds as qualified above (see P-10).

records.title: PREFERRED IUPAC NAMES
then: identify PINs with parenthetical PIN marker when applicable
distinguish preferred IUPAC names from acceptable general IUPAC names
examples: methane (preferred IUPAC name or PIN, a retained name)

It is sometimes convenient to employ parent hydrides of more complex structure, such as ring or ring-chain assemblies, for example biphenyl and styrene. The name ‘1,1′-biphenyl’ results from the application of Rule P-28.

A special class of parent structures having retained names (see P-12.3) is called functional parent compounds, for example, phenol and acetic acid. These two names are preferred IUPAC names; the corresponding systematic

records.source_quote: Preferred IUPAC names are names for structures or structural components that are preferred among two or more names for the same structure generated from two or more recommended IUPAC rules for organic compounds or the many synonyms that have been coined and used over the years.

records.title: PRESELECTED NAMES
then: identify PINs with parenthetical PIN marker when applicable
distinguish preferred IUPAC names from acceptable general IUPAC names
tables_or_terms: Table 2.1, with the exception of methane (carbane), are preselected names, and the concept is illustrated by the following examples
records.source_quote: Preselected names are names for structures or structural components chosen among two or more names for noncarbon-containing (inorganic) parents to be used as the basis for preferred IUPAC names for organic derivatives in the nomenclature of organic compounds.

records.title: RETAINED NAMES
then: identify PINs with parenthetical PIN marker when applicable
distinguish preferred IUPAC names from acceptable general IUPAC names
records.source_quote: Retained names are traditional or common, well-established names that may be either preferred IUPAC names, such as naphthalene, pyridine, and acetic acid; or preselected names, such as hydrazine and hydroxylamine; or as alternative names allowed in general nomenclature, for example, allene.

records.title: METHODOLOGY
then: identify PINs with parenthetical PIN marker when applicable
distinguish preferred IUPAC names from acceptable general IUPAC names
records.source_quote: In this book, names of parent structures, characteristic groups and their prefixes, and organic compounds are systematically identified as preferred IUPAC names, prefixes, and suffixes; or as preselected names, prefixes, or suffixes.

records.title: OPERATIONS IN NOMENCLATURE OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: The operations described in this section all involve structural modifications, and are classified first according to the type of modification, for example ‘replacement’; and then according to the way in which the modification is expressed, for example ‘by use of replacement infixes’.

records.title: THE SUBSTITUTIVE OPERATION
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: The substitutive operation involves the exchange of one or more hydrogen atoms for another atom or group of atoms.

records.source_quote: The additive operation involves the formal assembly of a structure from its component parts without loss of any atoms or groups.

records.source_quote: The prefix ‘nor’ is used to indicate removal of an unsubstituted saturated skeletal atom from a ring or a chain of a stereoparent structure with its attached hydrogen atom(s).

records.title: THE CONJUNCTIVE OPERATION
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: The conjunctive operation involves the formal construction of a name for a compound from the names of its components with subtraction of the same number of hydrogen atoms from each component at each site of the junction.

records.title: By juxtaposition of component names (conjunctive nomenclature)
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: This method is used by Chemical Abstracts Service. It is not recommended for constructing preferred IUPAC names; substitutive nomenclature is the recommended operation (see P-51).

records.title: Ring formation
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: The formation of a ring by means of a direct link between any two atoms of a parent structure with loss of one hydrogen atom from each is indicated by the prefix ‘cyclo’.

records.title: THE MULTIPLICATIVE OPERATION
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: This operation allows the expression of multiple occurrences of parent structures connected by a symmetrical multivalent structure.

records.title: In functional class nomenclature the multiplicative operation is used to name assemblies of identical parent structures linked by a bi- or multivalent functional class name
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote:
Examples:
dimethyl 1,4-phenylene dipropanedioate (PIN)
dimethyl butanedioylbis[oxy(2,1-phenylene)] dibutanedioate (PIN)

records.title: THE FUSION OPERATION
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: The fusion operation involves the union of two rings or ring systems so that atoms or atoms and bonds are common to each.

records.title: OPERATIONS USED ONLY IN THE NOMENCLATURE OF NATURAL PRODUCTS
then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
records.source_quote: In the nomenclature of natural products several prefixes are used to indicate the loss of a group, i.e., the exchange of a group for hydrogen.

records.source_quote: P-13.8.1.1 The prefix ‘de’ (not ‘des’), followed by the name of a group or atom (other than hydrogen), denotes removal (or loss) of that group and addition of the necessary hydrogen atoms, i.e., exchange of that group with hydrogen atoms.

then: transform parent structure or name component according to the specified nomenclature operation
examples: from 2 ‘OH’ groups in the same structure)
records.source_quote: Intramolecular ethers, formally arising by elimination of water from two hydroxy groups of a single molecule of a monosaccharide (aldose or ketose) or monosaccharide derivative, is indicated by the detachable prefix ‘anhydro’ preceded by a pair of locants identifying the two hydroxy groups involved.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: Rules described in this section are of general application for naming types of compounds and individual compounds.

records.title: BONDING NUMBER
then: assign bonding number and lambda convention when bonding number is nonstandard
records.source_quote: The concept of a standard valence state for an atom is fundamental to organic nomenclature.

records.title: Definition
then: assign bonding number and lambda convention when bonding number is nonstandard
records.source_quote: The bonding number ‘n’ of a skeletal atom is the sum of the total number of bonding equivalents (valence bonds) of that skeletal atom to adjacent skeletal atoms, if any, in a parent hydride and the number of hydrogen atoms.

records.title: Standard bonding numbers. The bonding number of a skeletal atom is standard when it has the value given in Table 1.3
then: assign bonding number and lambda convention when bonding number is nonstandard
records.source_quote:
Table 1.3 Standard bonding numbers for the elements of Groups 13, 14, 15, 16, and 17
Standard bonding
number (n)Element
3BAlGaInTl
4CSiGeSnPb
3NPAsSbBi
2OSSeTePo
1FClBrIAt

records.title: Nonstandard bonding numbers
then: assign bonding number and lambda convention when bonding number is nonstandard
records.source_quote: A nonstandard bonding number of a neutral skeletal atom of a parent hydride is indicated by the symbol ‘λn’, cited in conjunction with an appropriate locant.

records.title: MULTIPLICATIVE PREFIXES
then: select the applicable multiplicative prefix class and cite it with the multiplied feature
records.source_quote: Three types of multiplicative prefixes are used in names to denote multiplicity of identical features in structures (characteristic groups, substituent groups, multiple bonds) and correspondingly of affixes (suffixes, infixes, and prefixes) in names.

records.title: Basic multiplicative prefixes denote simple features and, in general, are the first choice among such prefixes to specify multiplicity (ref. 15). They are listed in Table 1.4

records.title: The prefix mono
then: select the applicable multiplicative prefix class and cite it with the multiplied feature
records.source_quote: P-14.2.1.1.1 When alone, the numerical term for the number ‘1’ is ‘mono’ and that for ‘2’ is ‘di’.

records.title: Derivation of basic numerical terms
then: select the applicable multiplicative prefix class and cite it with the multiplied feature
records.source_quote: After ‘undeca-’ (for the number eleven), composite numerical terms are formed systematically by citing the basic terms in the order opposite to that of the constituent digits in the arabic numbers.

records.title: Numerical terms for compound or complex features
then: select the applicable multiplicative prefix class and cite it with the multiplied feature
records.source_quote: Multiplicative prefixes for compound or complex entities, such as substituted substituents, are formed by adding the ending ‘kis’ to the basic multiplicative prefix ending in ‘a’, ‘tetrakis’, ‘pentakis’, etc.

records.title: Multiplicative prefixes for naming assemblies of identical units
then: select the applicable multiplicative prefix class and cite it with the multiplied feature
records.source_quote:
The traditional prefixes used to denote the number of repeated identical units in unbranched ring assemblies (see P-28) are as follows:
2bi 5quinque 8octi
3ter6sexi9novi
4quater7septi10deci
This list has been completed from 11 to 9999.

records.title: Types of locants
then: assign cite or omit locants according to locant necessity and ambiguity rules
records.source_quote: Traditional types of locants are arabic numbers, for example, 1, 2, 3; primed locants, for example, 1′, 1′′′, 2′′; locants including a lower case Roman letter, for example, 3a, 3b; italicized Roman letters, for example, O, N, P; Greek letters, for example, ‘α-, β-, γ-’ and compound locants, for example, ‘1(10)’ and ‘5(17)’.

records.title: Position of locants
then: assign cite or omit locants according to locant necessity and ambiguity rules
records.source_quote: Locants (numerals and/or letters) are placed immediately before that part of the name to which they relate, except in the case of the traditional contracted names when locants are placed at the front of their names.

records.title: Citation of locants
then: assign cite or omit locants according to locant necessity and ambiguity rules
records.source_quote: In preferred IUPAC names, if any locants are essential for defining the structure of the parent structure or of a unit of structure as defined by its appropriate enclosing marks, then all locants must be cited for the parent structure or that structural unit.

records.title: Omission of locants
then: assign cite or omit locants according to locant necessity and ambiguity rules
records.source_quote: The practice of omitting locants when there is no ambiguity is widespread. However, for absolute clarity in preferred IUPAC names it is necessary to be prescriptive about when omission of locants is permissible.

records.title: Lowest set of locants
then: assign cite or omit locants according to locant necessity and ambiguity rules
examples: Note: An exception must be noted in the field of carotenoid nomenclature, where 5,8,5′,8′- is used rather than 5,5′,8,8′- (see P-101.5.2); the latter would be recommended for systematic substitutive nomenclature
records.source_quote: The lowest set of locants is defined as the set that, when compared term by term with other locant sets, each cited in order of increasing value, has the lowest term at the first point of difference; for example, the locant set ‘2,3,5,8’ is lower than ‘3,4,6,8’ and ‘2,4,5,7’.

records.title: NUMBERING
then: apply numbering procedure for the selected parent structure or name component
examples: (b) indicated hydrogen for unsubstituted compounds; a higher locant may be needed at another position to accommodate a substituent suffix in accordance with structural feature (c);
(c) principal characteristic groups and free valences (suffixes);
(d) ‘added indicated hydrogen’ (consistent with the structure of the compound and in accordance with further substitution);
(i) low locants are given to hydro/dehydro prefixes (see first example and P-31.2.2) and ‘ene’ and ‘yne’ endings;
(ii) low locants are given first to multiple bonds as a set and then to double bonds (see second and third examples and P-31.1.1.1);
(f) detachable alphabetized prefixes, all considered together in a series of increasing numerical order;
(g) lowest locants for the substituent cited first as a prefix in the name;

Two important changes have been made to the 1979 recommendations (ref 1):
(1) heteroatoms in chains are now considered to be a part of the parent hydride; as such, they have seniority over suffixes for numbering as for heteroatoms in rings;
(2) hydro/dehydro prefixes are now classified as detach

records.title: ALPHANUMERICAL ORDER
then: order name components alphanumerically using the specified tie breakers
examples: [butyl is not treated as butan-1-yl; for the use of enclosing marks, see P-16.5.1.2]
[not 1,4-dimethyl-2,5,8-trichloronaphthalene; ‘trichloro’ is cited before ‘dimethyl’ as ‘chloro’ is earlier alphabetically than ‘methyl’]
[not 1,4,6-trichloro-5,8-dimethylnaphthalene; the locant set ‘1,2,4,5,8’ is lower than ‘1,4,5,6,8’ (see P-14.3.5)]
records.source_quote: Alphanumerical order has been commonly called ‘alphabetical order’. As these ordering principles do involve ordering both letters and numbers, in a strict sense, it is best called ‘alphanumerical order’ in order to convey the message that both letters and numbers are involved.

records.title: The name of a prefix for a substituent is considered to begin with the first letter of its complete name
then: order name components alphanumerically using the specified tie breakers
examples: the compound substituent name in the PIN starts with ‘d’, and ‘d’ is earlier alphabetically than ‘e’]
(‘dimethylpentyl’ begins with ‘d’ that precedes ‘e’ in alphabetical order)

records.title: When an alphanumerical ordering is required and Roman letters do not permit a decision for the order of citation, italicized letters are considered
then: order name components alphanumerically using the specified tie breakers
examples: (for use of enclosing marks, see P-16.5.1.2)
And similarly, naphtho[1,2-f]quinolin-2-yl is alphanumerically preferred to naphtho[1,2-g]quinolin-1-yl (‘f’ before ‘g’).
records.source_quote:
Examples:
3-tert-butyl-1-(1-methylpropyl)benzene
1-(butan-2-yl)-3-tert-butylbenzene (PIN)
(for use of enclosing marks, see P-16.5.1.2)
[not 1-sec-butyl-3-tert-butylbenzene]
3-(as-indacen-3-yl)-5-(s-indacen-1-yl)pyridine (PIN)
[not 5-s-indacen-1-yl-3-as-indacen-3-ylpyridine]
And similarly, naphtho[1,2-f]quinolin-2-yl is alphanumerically preferred to naphtho[1,2-g]quinolin-1-yl (‘f’ before ‘g’).

records.title: When two or more prefixes consist of identical Roman letters, priority for order of citation is given to the group that contains the lowest locant(s) at the first point of difference
then: order name components alphanumerically using the specified tie breakers
examples: (for ordering the substituents ‘2’ is lower than ‘3’; the fact that ‘N’ is lower than ‘4’ is irrelevant)
(the locant ‘2’ is lower than ‘3’; for the use of enclosing marks, see P-16.5.1.2)
the locant set ‘2,2’ is lower than ‘3,3’)
the locant set ‘2,3’ is lower than ‘3,2’]
records.source_quote:
Examples:
4-(2-methylbutyl)-N-(3-methylbutyl)aniline (PIN)
4-(2-methylbutyl)-N-(3-methylbutyl)benzenamine
(for ordering the substituents ‘2’ is lower than ‘3’; the fact that ‘N’ is lower than ‘4’ is irrelevant)

(the locant ‘1’ is lower than ‘2’)

(the locant ‘2’ is lower than ‘3’; for the use of enclosing marks, see P-16.5.1.2)
1-(2-methylpentan-2-yl)-

records.title: NONALPHANUMERICAL ORDER
then: order name components using the specified nonalphanumerical seniority or citation order
examples: alphanumerical characters are identical; at the fourth character of the name, the letter ‘a’ is preferred to an open parenthesis}
the alphabetic letters and the primary substituent locants are identical for both names;
however, there is no locant for the first internal substituent in the PIN, but in the
alternative name the locant for the first internal substituent is ‘4’ and no locant is preferred to ‘4’}
be a diamine; alphanumerical orders of the first two names are the same;
at the third character the nonalphanumerical ‘{’ is preferred to ‘[’]
records.source_quote: Alphanumerical ordering resolves practically all of the ordering problems for organic names.

records.title: INDICATED AND ‘ADDED INDICATED HYDROGEN’
then: add indicated hydrogen or added indicated hydrogen notation where required
examples: In the first example, the ‘indicated hydrogen’ locates one hydrogen atom in position ‘1’ of the pyrrole ring; and in the second, the ‘indicated hydrogen’ indicates an ‘extra’ hydrogen atom at position ‘3’ , i.e., one hyd
Detailed procedures for using ‘indicated hydrogen’ are discussed in P-58.2.1.
Detailed procedures for using ‘added indicated hydrogen’ are discussed in P-58.2.2.
records.source_quote: P-14.7.1 Indicated hydrogen. Under certain circumstances it is necessary to indicate in the name of a mancude ring or ring system, i.e., one that contains the maximum number of noncumulative double bonds, one or more positions where no multiple bond is attached.

records.title: Organic adducts
then: represent adduct components using the applicable organic or mixed organic-inorganic adduct convention
examples: Solvates, including hydrates (see P-14.8.2), are treated as adducts and preferred IUPAC names must use the notation for denoting the proportion of components described above. In general nomenclature, hydrates may be name
records.source_quote: An adduct is a chemical species, each molecular entity of which is formed by direct combination of separate molecular entities in such a way that there may be a change of connectivity but no loss of atoms from any of the molecular entities (see ref.

records.title: Mixed organic – inorganic adducts
then: represent adduct components using the applicable organic or mixed organic-inorganic adduct convention
records.source_quote: Formulas for mixed adducts composed of organic and inorganic chemical species are written in the following order: organic components in order as described in P-14.8.1, inorganic components in order as described in Ref 12; water (if present), is cited last.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: ‘Nomenclature’, in chemistry, is a system by which names are formed using various nomenclatural operations in accordance with a set of principles, rules, and conventions.

records.title: SUBSTITUTIVE NOMENCLATURE
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Substitutive nomenclature is based on the selection of a parent structure having substitutable hydrogen atoms (see P-15.1.5) which are substituted by nomenclaturally significant structural fragments represented either by prefixes and/or suffixes or only by prefixes.

records.title: Parent hydrides
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
examples: Parent hydrides may be substituted by prefixes and/or suffixes. A complete substitutive name may be described schematically in Fig. 1.1.
Fig. 1.1 Order of components in substitutive names based on parent hydrides
records.source_quote: Parent hydrides are unbranched acyclic, cyclic structures, or acyclic/cyclic structures to which only hydrogen atoms are attached.

records.title: Functional parent compounds
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
examples: A complete preferred IUPAC substitutive name based on a functional parent compound may be described schematically as shown in Fig. 1.2.
Fig. 1.2 Order of components in a substitutive name based on a functional parent compound
records.source_quote: P-15.1.2.1 Functional parent compounds used in systematic organic nomenclature are structures having retained names that imply the presence of at least one characteristic group and which have one or more hydrogen atoms attached to at least one of its skeletal atoms or in which at least one of its characteristic groups can form at least one kind of functional modification.

records.title: Suffixes (see also P-33)
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: There are, in general, two kinds of suffixes, functional suffixes and cumulative suffixes.

records.title: Position of the endings ‘ane’, ‘ene’, and ‘yne’
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: The modification of the ending ‘ane’ to ‘ene’ or ‘yne’ in acyclic, cyclic and polycyclic parent hydrides is used to describe the subtractive operation that inserts double and triple bonds into saturated parent hydrides.

records.title: Prefixes and their order in names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: There are two kinds of prefixes, nondetachable and detachable. Nondetachable prefixes describe structural modifications to the parent structure creating new parent structures, for example, replacement prefixes, which can be either skeletal replacement (‘a’) prefixes (see P-15.4) or ‘functional replacement prefixes’ (see P-15.5).

records.title: Detachable alphabetizable prefixes
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Nondetachable prefixes, the detachable saturation prefixes (‘hydro/dehydro’), and the detachable alphatizable prefixes are cited in names as indicated in Fig.

records.title: Nondetachable prefixes
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Nondetachable prefixes are permanently attached to the name of the parent structure in a given order, which normally matches the order of operations used to modify the parent structure.

records.title: Nondetachable prefixes creating new parent structures:
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants

records.title: Replacement of atoms other than hydrogen by other atoms
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: This type of replacement, called ‘skeletal replacement’, is essentially the replacement of carbon atoms by heteroatoms; it takes place with acyclic and cyclic hydrocarbons generating new parent structures by using skeletal replacement (‘a’) prefixes, i.e., ‘oxa’, ‘aza’, ‘thia’, etc.

records.title: Detachable hydro/dehydro prefixes
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: These two prefixes are introduced in names by an additive or subtractive operation; thus, they are not included in the category of detachable alphabetizable prefixes describing substitution (P-15.1.5.3).

records.title: Detachable alphabetizable prefixes
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: These prefixes describe substituent groups denoting characteristic groups that are not cited as principal characteristic group or groups derived from parent hydrides and are cited before the ‘hydro-dehydro’ prefixes, if present (see P-31.2), or nondetachable prefixes, if present, as indicated in Fig.

records.title: Other components of substitutive names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: In addition to the components described above, the following nomenclatural indicators are added, as required:

records.title: Construction of substitutive names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: This subsection describes the formation of substitutive names and the application of four general sets of rules dealing with numbering (P-14.4), locants (P-14.3), multiplicative prefixes (P-14.2), and alphanumerical order (P-14.5).

records.title: Naming of alkanes and branched alkanes
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
reject:
reject_name_form: 2,4,5-trimethylhexane;
the locant set 2,3,5 is lower than 2,4,5

reject_name_form: 3-ethyl-5-methylhexane;
the locant set 2,4 is lower than 3,5
examples: the locant set 2,3,5 is lower than 2,4,5)
the locant set 2,4 is lower than 3,5)
the lower locant must be assigned to the
substituent group that is cited first)
records.source_quote: P-15.1.7.1.1 The names of alkanes are either the retained names, methane, ethane, propane, and butane; or are names formed systematically by adding the ending ‘ane’ to a basic multiplicative prefix, with elision of the final letter ‘a’ of the multiplicative term (See Chapter P-2).

records.title: The numbering rules
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: The following examples illustrate the rule for numbering described in P-14.4. This rule establishes an order of priority among different nomenclatural features for assignment of the lowest possible locants.

records.title: Substitution rules for parent structures with retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: The following rules describe the substitutability of parent structures having retained names found in P-22.1.2, P-22.1.3, Tables 2.2, 2.3, 2.7, 2.8, Tables 3.1, 3.2, and for functional parent compounds given in P-34.

records.title: Type 2 Limited substitution generalized as follows:
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 1 retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Type 1 retained names of parent hydrides described in Chapters P-2 and P-3 have unlimited substitution by substituent groups cited either as suffixes or prefixes.

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 2 retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Substitution of parent hydrides with retained names and of functional parent compounds whose retained names explicitly or implicitly express the presence of a characteristic group normally expressed as a suffix, such as ‘-one’, or a functional class, such as ‘ether’, is limited in different ways as described in the following subsections.

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 2a retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Type 2a retained names include functional parent compounds whose name expresses or implies a characteristic group expressed as suffix in systematic names.

records.title: Substitution by substituent groups, expressed as prefixes, having a lower seniority than that denoted by the suffix is allowed
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
examples: A suffix explicitly or implicitly present cannot be expressed as a prefix;
records.source_quote:
Examples:
H2N-CH2-CO-CH3
aminoacetone
1-aminopropan-2-one (PIN)
HS-CH2-COOH
sulfanylacetic acid (PIN)
A suffix explicitly or implicitly present cannot be expressed as a prefix;
Examples:
HOOC-CH2-COOH
propanedioic acid (PIN)
malonic acid
(not 2-carboxyacetic acid)
benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid (PIN)
phthalic acid
(not 2-carboxybenzoic acid)
benzene-1,2,4-tricarboxylic acid (PIN)
(not 2,4-dicarboxybenzoic acid;
not 4-carboxyphthalic acid)

records.title: The senior characteristic group or functional class name must be expressed in the name
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote:
Examples:
2-hydroxybenzoic acid (PIN)
(not 2-carboxyphenol)
phenylnitrous amide (PIN)
N-nitrosoaniline

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 2b retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Type 2b retained names include parent compounds explicitly or implicitly devoid of suffixes; acetylene (PIN) and allene are examples.

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 2c retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: Type 2c retained names are functional parent compounds that are not included in Type 2b, for example, hydroxylamine (see P-68.3.1.1.1), formic acid (see P-65.1.8), and anisole (see P-34.1.1.4).

records.title: Substitution rules for Type 3 retained names
then: assemble substitutive name from parent structure plus nondetachable prefixes detachable prefixes suffixes endings and locants
records.source_quote: No substitution of any kind is allowed for retained names of parent hydrides and functional parent compounds of type 3.

records.title: Introduction
then: assemble functional class name using substituent group name or functional modifier according to functional class seniority
records.source_quote: Functional class nomenclature was quite important in the early days of organic chemistry when many compounds were named using class names.

records.title: Functional class nomenclature using substituent group names
then: assemble functional class name using substituent group name or functional modifier according to functional class seniority
records.source_quote: P-15.2.1.1 Functional class names are formed by expressing the functional class name of the compound as one word and the remainder of the molecule as a monovalent group name cited as a separate word, placed in front of the class name.

records.title: Functional class nomenclature using functional modifiers
then: assemble functional class name using substituent group name or functional modifier according to functional class seniority
examples: Functional modifiers are still acceptable for general nomenclature purposes, but the preferred IUPAC names are substitutive names for azines, oximes, hydrazones, semicarbazones, carbohydrazones, acetals, and hemiacetals.
(the term ‘oxime’ is added to the name of the carbonyl compound)
records.source_quote: Many derivatives of principal characteristic groups or functional parent compounds (see P-34) may be named by modifiers consisting of one or more separate words placed after the name of the parent structure.

records.title: Seniority of functional classes
then: assemble functional class name using substituent group name or functional modifier according to functional class seniority
examples: (an alcohol is senior to a halide)
(an acyl chloride senior to a nitrile)
records.source_quote: When two classes are present and both named by functional class nomenclature, the senior class must be chosen in accordance with the seniority order of classes (see P-41).

records.title: Polyfunctional compounds
then: assemble functional class name using substituent group name or functional modifier according to functional class seniority
records.source_quote: Functional class names for polyfunctional compounds are not recommended. Substitutive nomenclature is preferred.

records.title: Introduction
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints
records.source_quote: Multiplicative nomenclature is used to name assemblies of identical parent structures.

records.title: Identical structural units
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints

records.title: Multiplicative substituent groups
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints
records.source_quote: There are two types of multiplicative substituent groups:

records.title: Multiplicative name formation
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints
records.source_quote: Multiplicative names are formed in accordance with the number of occurrences of identical structural units as defined in P-15.3.1.1 and the relationship of the linking multiplicative substituent group to the identical structural units.

records.title: Assemblies of identical structural units (see P-15.3.1.1)
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints

[Note: A double set of square brackets appears in the above name because square brackets are required for substituent names derived from ring assembly names (see P-16.5.2.1) and brackets are needed to enclose the multipl

records.source_quote: Retained and systematic names can be used as identical parent structural units. Primes, double primes, etc., are used to distinguish among the locants of the identical parent structural unit.

records.title: In multiplicative nomenclature, the use of primes on the italic letter N is used to differentiate among nitrogen atoms of identical structural units that contain one characteristic group with one or more nitrogen atoms

records.title: Use of multiplicative prefixes ‘bis’, ‘tris’, etc
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints
records.source_quote: Identical parent structures for which the numerical prefixes ‘di-’, ‘tri-’, etc. cannot be used because of potential ambiguity (see P-16.3.6) are enclosed in parentheses or square brackets, as required, and preceded by the numerical terms ‘bis-’, ‘tris-’, etc.

records.title: Substituted identical parent structures (see P-15.3.2.1)
then: use multiplicative nomenclature only when multiplied units and multiplying groups satisfy identity and symmetry constraints

records.source_quote: P-15.4.2.1 Skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature is applied simply in the generation of individual heteromonocyclic parent hydrides (see P-22.2.3), heterocyclic von Baeyer parent hydrides (see P-23.3), heterocyclic spiro parent hydrides composed of only monocyclic rings (see P-24.2.4.1), and fullerenes (see P-27.5).

then: apply skeletal replacement nomenclature by replacing skeletal atoms and marking replacement prefixes
records.source_quote: P-15.4.3.1 Skeletal replacement names are formed by placing skeletal replacement (‘a’) prefixes in front of the name of the unbranched parent structure in accord with their seniority order given in P-15.4.1.2.

records.source_quote: Functional replacement nomenclature is a method by which oxygen atoms of characteristic groups and in functional parent compounds are replaced by halogen, chalcogen, and/or nitrogen atoms.

then: apply additive or subtractive modification when parent hydride hydrogenation state changes
records.source_quote: The replacement of oxygen atoms or hydroxy groups by other atoms or groups can be described by nondetachable prefixes attached to, or infixes inserted into, names of characteristic groups, parent hydrides and functional parent compounds having retained or systematic names.

records.title: Replacement in characteristic groups expressed as suffixes in substitutive nomenclature

examples: thioamide (preferred suffix; no contraction to thiamide)

records.source_quote: Replacement in suffixes is limited to –OO–, =S and –S–, =Se and –Se–, =Te and –Te–, =NH and =NNH2 and any combination of these affixes (see Table 1.6).

records.title: Replacement in characteristic groups expressed as prefixes in substitutive nomenclature
then: apply additive or subtractive modification when parent hydride hydrogenation state changes
records.source_quote: Functional replacement is used to modify prefixes containing oxygen atoms by the prefixes ‘thio’, ‘seleno’, and ‘telluro’.

records.source_quote: Functional replacement is used to modify carboxylic acids and oxoacids, by prefixes and suffixes according to specific rules given in P-15.5.3.4.1 and P-15.5.3.4.2 below.

records.title: Introduction
then: construct conjunctive name by combining parent hydride and principal characteristic group component as specified
records.source_quote: Conjunctive nomenclature is based essentially on the conjunctive operation, by which a compound is formally constructed by subtracting the same number of hydrogen atoms from each component at each site where joined.

records.title: Names are formed by juxtaposition of component names. The name of the cyclic component is cited first followed by the systematic or retained name of the component to which the principal characteristic group is attached
then: construct conjunctive name by combining parent hydride and principal characteristic group component as specified

Examples:
cyclohexanemethanol
cyclohexylmethanol (PIN)
benzeneacetic acid
phenylacetic acid (PIN)
P-15.6.1.2 When necessary, the position of attachment of the side chain to the cyclic component is given by the appropriate locant placed before the name of the cyclic component, unless locants for structural features referring to the cyclic component, such as heteroatoms and indicated hydrogen, are already there; in this case the locant follows the

records.title: When different side chains are attached to a cyclic component:
then: construct conjunctive name by combining parent hydride and principal characteristic group component as specified
examples: (a carboxylic acid is senior to an alcohol)
records.source_quote:
(a) the chain that contains the principal characteristic group is chosen for naming by the conjunctive method; or
(b) if there is more than one side chain containing the principal characteristic group, the conjunctive name that expresses the greater number of the principal characteristic group is chosen.

records.title: When the side chain is linked to two different cyclic components, the senior ring or ring system is selected in accord with the seniority order of rings and ring systems (see P-44.2)
then: construct conjunctive name by combining parent hydride and principal characteristic group component as specified

records.source_quote: P-16.5.2.1 Brackets enclose descriptors denoting fusion sites in fused ring systems and enclose numbers denoting the length of bridges and chains connecting spiro atoms in names of polyalicyclic ring systems constructed according to von Baeyer methods.

records.title: Brackets enclose locants that describe structural features of components, such as double bonds in bridges and heteroatoms of component rings in names of fused ring systems

records.source_quote:
Examples:
6,7-(epiprop[1]en[1]yl[3]ylidene)benzo[a]cyclohepta[e][8]annulene (PIN, P-25.4.3.4.2)
5H-pyrido[2,3-d][1,2]oxazine [PIN, P-25.3.2.4 (d)]

P-16.5.4.1.3 In determining the nesting order in a name the parentheses used to indicate compound locants, phane ampl

records.title: ITALICIZATION
then: apply orthographic formatting to locants prefixes suffixes punctuation elision and inserted vowels
records.source_quote: Italicizing marks letters that are not involved in the primary stage of alphanumerical ordering.

records.title: Lower case italic letters are used in descriptors for fusion sites in names of fused ring systems
then: apply orthographic formatting to locants prefixes suffixes punctuation elision and inserted vowels

Example:
selenopheno[2,3-b]selenophene (PIN, P-25.3.1.3)
The letters o, m, and p have been used in place of ortho, meta, and para, respectively, to designate the 1,2-, 1,3-, and 1,4- isomers of disubstituted benzene.

records.title: Italicized elements symbols, such as O, N, As, are locants indicating attachment of a substituent to these heteroatoms

Examples:
N,N-diethylethanamine (PIN, P-62.2.2.1)

records.title: Italic terms, syllables, and capital Roman letters are used in some structural descriptors and stereodescriptors, such as ‘cis’, ‘trans’, ‘R’, ‘S’, ‘E’, ‘Z’, ‘r’, ‘s’, ‘c’, ‘t’, and ‘retro’
then: apply orthographic formatting to locants prefixes suffixes punctuation elision and inserted vowels

records.title: Vowels are systematically elided as follows:
then: apply orthographic formatting to locants prefixes suffixes punctuation elision and inserted vowels
examples: (b) in Hantzsch-Widman names, the final letter ‘a’ of an ‘a’ prefix when followed by a vowel;
(c) the terminal letter ‘a’ in the names of numerical multiplicative prefixes when followed by a suffix beginning with ‘a’ or ‘o’;
(d) the terminal letter ‘a’ of an element ‘a’ prefix in ‘a(ba)n’ repeating unit names when followed by a vowel;
(e) the terminal letter ‘o’ of a functional replacement infix when followed by a vowel;
(f) the terminal letter ‘o’ of ‘benzo’ in names of ‘benzoheterocycles’ formed by fusion of a benzene ring to a heteromonocycle whose name begins with a vowel [an exception to P-16.7.2 (g)].
records.source_quote:
(a) the terminal letter ‘e’ in names of parent hydrides or endings ‘ene’ and ‘yne’ when followed by a suffix or ending beginning with ‘a’, ‘e’, ‘i’, ‘o’, ‘u’, or ‘y’;
Examples:
pentanal (PIN, P-66.6.1.1.1)
cyclopentadec-1-en-4-yne (PIN, P-31.1.3.1)
methanium (PIN, P-73.1.1.2)
butan-2-one (PIN, P-64.2.2.1)
tetramethylboranuide (PIN, P-72.3)
sulfanyl (preselected prefix, P-29.3.1)
(b) in Hantzsch-Widman names, the final letter ‘a’ of an ‘a’ prefix

records.title: There is no elision of terminal vowels in the following cases:
then: apply orthographic formatting to locants prefixes suffixes punctuation elision and inserted vowels
examples: (b) from replacement or numerical multiplicative prefixes in skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature;
(c) from multiplicative prefixes in homogeneous acyclic parent hydrides other than hydrocarbons and boron hydrides;
(d) from numerical multiplicative prefixes in multiplicative parent compounds;
(e) from numerical multiplicative prefixes before substituent prefix names;
(f) from component prefixes of compound (see P-29.1.2) and complex (see P-29.1.3) prefixes before following prefixes beginning with a vowel;

(g) from prefixes designating attached components in fusion nomenclature; for example, the terminal letter ‘o’ of acenaphtho, benzo, perylo, phenanthro, and the terminal letter ‘a’ of anthra, cyclopropa, cyclobuta, are n
This recommendation is in accordance with P-25.3.1.3 and Rule R-2.4.1.1 of the 1993 Guide (ref. 2) that abrogated the elision recommended by Rule A-21.4 in the 1979 edition (ref. 1).
records.source_quote:
(a) in conjunctive names;
Example:
benzeneacetic acid (P-15.6.1.1)
[phenylacetic acid (PIN)]
(b) from replacement or numerical multiplicative prefixes in skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature;
Example:
2,4,6,8-tetrasilaundecan-11-yl (preferred prefix, P-15.4.3.2.2)
(c) from multiplicative prefixes in homogeneous acyclic parent hydrides other than hydrocarbons and boron hydrides;
Example:
nonaazane (P-21.2.2)
(not nonazane)
(d) from numerical mu

Examples:

(not ethanesulfondiimidic acid)

records.title: For euphonic reasons, the letter ‘a’ is inserted between the root of the name for polyenes, polyynes, and polyenynes, and the numerical multiplicative prefix ‘di’, ‘tri’, etc., preceding the ending ‘ene’ or ‘yne’

Examples:
