records.title: Seniority order of suffixes
records.source_quote: The order of seniority of ketonic suffixes is C=O > C=S > C=Se > C=Te. Lowest locants are assigned in accordance with that order.

records.title: POLYFUNCTIONAL KETONES, PSEUDOKETONES AND HETERONES
records.source_quote: P-64.7.1 Ketones, pseudoketones and heterones, and their chalcogen analogues in the order =O > =S > =Se > =Te, are senior to hydroxy compounds and their chalcogen analogues, amines, and imines in the seniority order of classes. In the presence of a...

records.title: When an oxygen atom of a ketone, pseudoketone, or heterone is replaced by a sulfur, selenium or tellurium atom, the seniority order for expression as the suffix is O > S > Se > Te
records.source_quote: Example: 1-[3-(2-sulfanylidenebutyl)cyclohexyl]butan-2-one (PIN) 1-[3-(2-thioxobutyl)cyclohexyl]butan-2-one

records.title: ACYLOINS
records.source_quote: α-Hydroxy ketones, RCH(OH)-CO-R, in which R is an alkyl, aryl, or a heterocyclic group, have the class name 'acyloins' and are named by substitutive nomenclature as substituted ketones, in accordance with the seniority order: ketones > hydroxy compounds...

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: This Chapter includes acids named substitutively by means of suffixes, that is, carboxylic acids, sulfonic, sulfinic, and analogous selenium and tellurium acids. Their derivatives, such as esters, acyl halides, and anhydrides, are also included. Salts are...

records.title: CARBOXYLIC ACIDS AND FUNCTIONAL REPLACEMENT ANALOGUES
records.source_quote: Carboxylic acids have the structure R-C(=O)-OH, where R can be a hydrogen atom. Nitrogenous analogues are carboxylic acids in which =O has been replaced by =NH, =NNH2, =N-OH, or in which -OH has been replaced by -NH-OH. Chalcogen analogues are carboxylic...

records.title: Retained names
records.source_quote: Carboxylic acids derived from natural sources were often given trivial names reminiscent of their animal or vegetable origin. In both 1979 and 1993, the list of these trivial names was significantly reduced, systematic names being recommended.

records.title: Retained names as preferred IUPAC names

records.source_quote: When required, numbering is based on the seniority order given in P-14.4.

records.title: Functional replacement in peroxycarboxylic acids

records.title: Amic, anilic, and aldehydic acids
records.source_quote: Amic acids are compounds containing both a carboxy, -COOH, and a carboxamide, -CONH2, group; similarly, anilic and aldehydic acids include both a carboxy group and a carboxanilide, -CO-NH-C6H5, or formyl, -CHO, group, respectively. The endings 'amic acid',...

examples: (PIN) 3-carbamoylpropanoic acid 3-(aminocarbonyl)propanoic acid succinamic acid H2N-CO-COOH oxamic acid (PIN; a retained name) (not oxalamic acid)

records.title: Anilic acids

records.title: Aldehydic acids

records.title: Mixed acyl groups
records.source_quote: Mixed acyl groups of the type -(C=X)-[CH2]x-(C=Y)- are named by substitution of alkanediyl substituent groups.

records.title: Formic acid
records.source_quote: For the purpose of organic nomenclature, formic acid is considered to be a monocarboxylic acid (see P-65.1). It is a retained name, treated like acetic acid, can be functionalized leading to salts, esters, and anhydrides, and forms an acyl group that is...

examples: (PIN) H{S/O}C-COOH (thiocarboxy)formic acid (PIN, see P-65.1.5.2) P-65.1.8.3 Acyl groups derived from formic acid are formed as described in P-65.1.7.2 and compound prefixes are formed in accordance with the structure of the substituent group. Examples: Cl-CO- carbonochloridoyl (preferred prefix) (not chloroformyl) Br-CS- carbonobromidothioyl (preferred prefix) [not bromo(thioformyl)] HCO-O- formyloxy (preferred prefix) HCO-S- formylsulfanyl (preferred prefix)

records.title: Carbonic acid
records.source_quote: The nomenclature of chalcogen analogues and derivatives of carbonic acid is based on functional replacement of one oxygen in -OH groups or of the doubly bonded oxygen atom, =O, and is indicated by infixes. Substitution of formic acid is not recommended for...

records.title: The contracted name 'carbamic acid' (from carbonamidic acid), for H2N-CO-OH, and 'carbamimidic acid' (from carbonamidimidic acid), for H2N-C(=NH)-OH, are retained and are the preferred IUPAC names
examples: (PIN) P-65.2.1.2 Functional replacement in carbonic acid and carbamic acid names by -OO-, -S-, -Se-, and -Te- is expressed by the infixes 'peroxo', 'thio', 'seleno', and 'telluro', respectively. Tautomeric groups in mixed chalcocarbonic acids, such as HO-CO-SH or HO-CS-OH, are distinguished by prefixing italic element symbols, such as 'S' or 'O', respectively, to the term 'acid'; the italic symbols 'OS' and 'SO' are used for peroxy acids. Contrary to Rules P-65.1.3 through P-65.1.5, functional replacement nomenclature is applied to the retained name 'carbamic acid' and not to the systematic name 'carbonamidic acid'. Examples:

records.title: Acyl groups derived from carbonic and related acids
records.source_quote: Acyl groups derived from carbonic and related acids, including functional replacement analogues, by the removal of one or two hydroxy groups from the acid are named in accordance with the methodology described in P-65.1.7.2. Names are formed in two ways:...

records.title: The prefix 'carboxy' and prefixes for chalcogen analogues
records.source_quote: The prefix 'carboxy' for -COOH is a retained prefix. Chalcogen analogues are named by functional replacement nomenclature provided that it is not necessary to specify the location of the chalcogen atom. Specification of chalcogen atoms is accomplished by...

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of the 'carbonoperoxoyl' prefix, -CO-OOH, are named in three ways:
examples: HOS-CSe- (2) (hydroxysulfanyl)carbonoselenoyl (preferred prefix) (3) (OS-thiohydroperoxy)carboselenoyl HOS-CO- or HSO-CO- (1) carbono(thioperoxoyl) (preferred prefix) (3) (thiohydroperoxy)carbonyl HS-O-CO-O- (2) [(sulfanyloxy)carbonyl]oxy (preferred prefix) (3) [(SO-thiohydroperoxy)carbonyl]oxy HSS-CO-O- (2) (disulfanylcarbonyl)oxy (preferred prefix) (3) [(dithiohydroperoxy)carbonyl]oxy
records.source_quote: (1) by using an infix when the position of the chalcogen atoms is not known; (2) by compound prefixes formed by concatenation; (3) by thiohydroperoxy prefixes using the italic prefixes SO- or OS-, as necessary. Methods (1) or (2) lead to preferred IUPAC names.

records.title: Names of nonacyl substituent groups derived from carbonic acid

records.title: Cyanic acid
records.source_quote: Cyanic acid is the retained name for NC-OH. The functional replacement name based on carbonic acid would be carbononitridic acid, but this name has not been used and, although systematic, is only recommended for general nomenclature. Cyanic acid is...

records.title: Di-, tri-, tetra-, and polycarbonic acids
records.source_quote: Di-, tri-, tetra-, and polycarbonic acids belong to the series of homopolynuclear acids, whose central atom is carbon. Their generic formula is HO-[CO-O]n-H, where n is 2, 3, 4, etc. and they are named by adding a multiplying prefix corresponding to the...

records.title: General methodology
records.source_quote: Nomenclature for functional analogues of the di-, tri-, tetra-, and polycarbonic acids follows the principles for naming polynuclear inorganic oxo acids (see P-67.2.1). Prefixes are used to indicate functional replacement and the chain is numbered...

records.title: Replacement by -OO-, -S-, =S, -Se-, =Se, -Te-, =Te, -NH-, =NH, and =NHNH2
records.source_quote: Functional replacement of oxygen atom(s), -OH, =O, -O-, is denoted by prefixes, i.e., peroxy for -OO-; thio for -S- or =S; seleno for -Se- or =Se; telluro for -Te-or =Te; imido for -NH- or =NH, and hydrazono for =NHNH2. The position of each replaced oxygen...

records.title: Superscripted italic letter locants N2, N3, etc. are used to designate substitution on nitrogen atoms that are not amide linkages that are part of the chain for which arabic numbers are used as locants
records.source_quote: This is a change. Primed letter locants, N', N'', N''', etc. were previously used as locants for nitrogen atoms that are not amide linkages that are part of the chain for which arabic numbers are used as locants.

records.title: Compound substituent groups may have to be used to name chalcogen analogues when the location of chalcogen atoms is not known

examples: (PIN) 2-imido-1-isocyanatodicarbonic acid (locants are used to avoid ambiguity)
records.source_quote: Prefixes bromo for -Br, chloro for -Cl, fluoro for -F, iodo for -I , azido for -N3, isocyano for -NC, and isocyanato for -NCO (and chalcogen analogues) are used to indicate functional replacement.

records.title: Replacement by NH2 and NHNH2 groups

records.source_quote: Suffixes are modified by the infixes 'thio', for -S- or =S, 'seleno', for -Se- or =Se, and 'telluro', for -Te- or =Te, and used as such. Tautomers are denoted by symbols S, Se, and Te placed in front of the term 'acid', to express positions of chalcogen...

records.title: Imidic and hydrazonic acids derived from sulfonic, sulfinic, etc. acids
records.source_quote: Imidic acids and hydrazonic acids derived from sulfonic, sulfinic, etc., acids are named by using suffixes such as 'sulfinimidic acid' for -S(=NH)-OH, 'sulfonohydrazonic acid' for -S(O)(=NNH2)-OH. The prefix 'di' is used to indicate the replacement of two...

records.title: Hydroximic and hydroxamic acids derived from sulfonic, sulfinic, etc. acids
records.source_quote: Hydroximic acids and hydroxamic acids derived from sulfonic, sulfinic, etc. acids are named as N-hydroxysulfonimidic acids and N-hydroxysulfonamides, etc. (see P-66.1.1.3.2), respectively.

records.title: Substitutive nomenclature, prefix mode for sulfonic, sulfinic, etc. acids
records.source_quote: P-65.3.2.1 When another group is also present that has seniority for citation as principal group (see P-41, P-42, P-43), or when all groups cannot be expressed as suffixes, organic oxoacids of sulfur, selenium or tellurium are named by adding to the name...

records.title: Acyl groups derived from sulfonic, sulfinic, etc. acids and their functional replacement analogues
tables_or_terms: -EOx-, where E = S, Se, or Te, x = 1 or 2, and R, R
records.source_quote: P-65.3.2.2.1 Acyl sulfonic, sulfinic, selenonic, seleninic, telluronic, and tellurinic groups, R-EOx-, -OxE-R-EOx- or -OxE-R-[R'-EOx-]-R''-EOx-, where E = S, Se, or Te, x = 1 or 2, and R, R', and R'' are chains, rings, or ring systems; their functional...

records.title: Substituent groups formed by concatenation
records.source_quote: When the name of an acyl group cannot be derived directly from that of the acid expressed by a suffix, a concatenation procedure is used. For this procedure names of divalent mononuclear acyl groups are required. Acyl groups corresponding to sulfuric and...

records.title: Polyfunctional compounds
records.source_quote: Polyfunctional compounds are named in accordance with the general order of seniority of suffixes described in Sections P-41 and P-43. When required, numbering is based on the seniority order described in P-61.1.

records.title: General methodology
records.source_quote: Acyl group names that are described in preceding sections are used unchanged to denote substituent groups. Thus, the traditional way of using acyl groups derived from acyclic carboxylic acids to name ketones, pseudoketones, and heterones is maintained (see...

records.title: Acyl halides from suffix acids
records.source_quote: Acyl halides in which hydroxy groups of all acid groups expressed as the suffix denoting the principal characteristic group (carboxylic, sulfonic, sulfinic, selenonic, etc. acids) have been replaced by halogen atoms (F, Cl, Br, and I) are named by citing...

records.title: Acyl pseudohalides from suffix acids
records.source_quote: P-65.5.2.1 Acyl pseudohalides in which hydroxy groups of all acid groups expressed as the suffix denoting the principal characteristic group (carboxylic, sulfonic, sulfinic, selenonic, etc. acids) have been replaced by pseudohalogen groups (N3, CN, NC,...

records.title: Acyl halides and pseudohalides from carbonic, cyanic, and polycarbonic acids
records.source_quote: P-65.5.3.1 Acyl groups derived from carbonic acid, carbamic acid, and related acids, such as 'carbonyl' from carbonic acid, 'carbamoyl' from carbamic acid, and 'carbamimidoyl' from carbamimidic acid are used to form the names of the corresponding acyl halides.

records.title: Acyl halides and pseudohalides from cyanic acid are formed in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) as acyl halides or pseudohalides of carbononitridic acid; (2) by citing the name of the halide or pseudohalide after the name of the acid. Method (1) generates preferred IUPAC names.

records.title: Acyl halides and pseudohalides as substituent groups
records.source_quote: When another group is present that has priority for citation as principal group or when attached to another substituting group, an acyl halide or pseudohalide is expressed: (1) by a prefix formed from the name of the acid, for example, 'carbonochloridoyl';...

records.title: General methodology
records.source_quote: Neutral salts and esters are both named using the name of the anion derived from the name of the acid. Anion names are formed by changing an '-ic acid' ending of an acid name to '-ate' and an '-ous acid' ending of an acid name to '-ite'. Then, salts are...

records.title: Salts
records.source_quote: P-65.6.2.1 Neutral salts of acids are named by citing the name of the cation(s) followed by the name of the anion (see P-72.2.2.2) as a separate word. Different cations are cited in alphabetical order. Formation of salts is a functionalization and not a...

records.title: Cyclic salts are named as heterocycles
records.source_quote: Example: 3,3'-spirobi[[2,4,3]benzodioxaplumbepine]-1,1',5,5'-tetrone (PIN)

records.title: Acid salts of polybasic organic acids are named in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) by substitutive nomenclature in which the free acid is cited as a prefix to the name of the anion; (2) in the same way as the neutral salts, the remaining acid hydrogen atom(s) being indicated by the word 'hydrogen' (preceded by a numerical prefix,...

records.title: Preferred IUPAC names of acid salts of organic derivatives of polybasic inorganic oxoacids (including carbonic) are named by method (2)
examples: (PIN) Note: In the nomenclature of inorganic chemistry (IR-8.4, ref. 12), the term 'hydrogen' is written directly in front of the name of the anion, without a space, to indicate that it is part of the anion. Examples: P(O)(O-)3- Na+ 2 H+ sodium dihydrogenphosphate
records.source_quote: Examples: HO-CO-O- Na+ sodium hydrogen carbonate (PIN) CH3-P(O)(O-)2 K+ H+ potassium hydrogen methylphosphonate (PIN) Note: In the nomenclature of inorganic chemistry (IR-8.4, ref. 12), the term 'hydrogen' is written directly in front of the name of the...

records.title: Definitions
records.source_quote: P-65.6.3.1.1 Esters of organic oxoacids, R-C(O)-O-R' (R can be H) or R-S(O)x-O-R (R ≠ H) or chalcogen analogues, are compounds formally derived from an organic oxoacid R-C(O)-OH (R can be H) or R-S(O)x-OH (R ≠ H) and an alcohol, phenol, heterol, or enol by...

records.title: All preferred IUPAC names for esters are named by functional class nomenclature
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CO-O-CH2-CH3 ethyl acetate (PIN) CH3-O-CO-CH2-CH2-CO-O-CH2-CH3 ethyl methyl butanedioate (PIN) methyl cyclohexanecarboxylate (PIN) methyl 4-ethylbenzene-1-sulfonate (PIN)

records.title: Multiplicative functional class names for esters
records.source_quote: In functional class nomenclature for esters the multiplicative operation (P-13.6.2) is used to name assemblies of identical parent anionic components linked by a di- or multivalent 'hydroxylic' component. A change occurs in the multiplicative operation...

records.title: Esters cited as prefixes
tables_or_terms: is preferred to the contracted name
records.source_quote: When, in an ester with the general structure R-CO-O-R' or R-S(O)x-O-R', another group is present that has priority for citation as the principal group or when all ester groups cannot be described by the methods prescribed for naming esters, an ester group...

records.title: Monoesters
records.source_quote: Monoesters formed from a monobasic acid and a 'monohydroxylic' component are named systematically by placing the 'hydroxylic' component denoted by an organyl group (alkyl, aryl, etc.) in front of the name of the acid component expressed as an anion derived...

records.title: Polyesters derived from a single acid component
records.source_quote: P-65.6.3.3.2.1 Fully esterified acids derived from a single acid are systematically named by placing the name(s) of the hydroxylic component denoted by an organyl group(s) (alkyl, aryl, etc.) as separate word(s) in front of the name of the acid component...

records.title: Esters of acid components whose preferred IUPAC names are derived using multiplicative nomenclature are named by two methods:
examples: (PIN) (not bis{2-[(4-methoxy-4-oxobutanoyl)oxy]phenyl} butanedioate; the PIN expresses two parent dicarboxylic acids) (2) butanedioylbis(oxy-2,1-phenylene) bis(methyl butanedioate) P-65.6.3.3.2.2.2 Esters that do not qualify for multiplicative names as described above are named as monoesters and other ester components are expressed as prefixes by substitutive nomenclature. A multiplicative name could be used in general nomenclature [see P-15.3.2 and P-51.3.3]. Examples:

(PIN) (not ethyl 3-[4-chloro-3-(methoxycarbonyl)phenoxy]benzoate; the parent structure of the PIN has more substituents) 1'-ethyl 1-methyl 6-chloro-3,3'-oxydibenzoate (this multiplicative name is acceptable only in general nomenclature.
records.source_quote: (1) all organyl components representing the 'hydroxylic' components are cited in front of the name of the multiplied acid component; (2) for esters where both organyl components representing the 'hydroxylic' component are exactly the same are cited with...

records.title: Polyesters formed from a single 'alcoholic' component
records.source_quote: Esters derived from a single 'polyhydroxylic' component are named by placing the name of the 'polyhydroxylic' component denoted by a multivalent organyl group (alkyl, aryl, etc.) in front of the name(s) of the acid component denoted by the anion names...

records.title: When anions are different, two methods are used:
records.source_quote: (1) names of anions are cited in alphanumerical order preceded by a locant, when required; multiplicative prefixes are used to denote a multiplicity of identical anionic components; (2) one anion is chosen as principal anion and all other ester groups are...

records.title: Polyesters derived from multiple acids and multiple 'alcoholic' components
records.source_quote: Multiplicative nomenclature, skeletal replacement ('a') nomenclature, or phane nomenclature is used when specific conditions for their use exist and are fulfilled.

records.title: Polyester names formed by using functional class multiplicative nomenclature
records.source_quote: Symmetrical esters are named by including the organyl constituent in the multiplied anion component name. When this condition is not fulfilled, in unsymmetrical esters, the organyl constituents are cited at the beginning of the name, in alphanumerical order.

records.title: Polyester names formed by using substitutive nomenclature, and/or multiplicative nomenclature, and functional class nomenclature
records.source_quote: Polyesters that cannot be named by functional class multiplicative nomenclature as described above, are named by using substitutive nomenclature to generate the names of the organyl substituents and those of the anions. When required, seniority of rings,...

records.title: Polyester names formed by using functional class nomenclature and skeletal replacement ('a') nomenclature:
examples: (PIN) [a functional class name in which the anion segment is named by skeletal replacement ('a') nomenclature.]
records.source_quote: Examples: dimethyl 3,8,10,15-tetraoxo-4,7,11,14-tetraoxaheptadecane-1,17-dioate (PIN) [a functional class name in which the anion segment is named by skeletal replacement ('a') nomenclature.] dimethyl methylenebis(carbonyloxyethane-2,1-diyl)...

records.title: Polyester names formed by using functional class nomenclature and phane nomenclature:
records.source_quote: Examples: phenyl 3,6,9-trioxo-2,5,8-trioxa-1,10(1),4,7(1,3)-tetrabenzenadecaphane-13-carboxylate (PIN) dimethyl 2,7,9,14,16,21-hexaoxo-3,6,10,13,17,20-hexaoxa-1,22(1),8,15(1,3)-tetrabenzenadocosaphane-13,223-dicarboxylate (PIN)

records.title: Partial esters of polybasic acids and their salts are named by two methods:
records.source_quote: (1) substitutively on the basis of the anion, the free acid group(s) and the ester group(s) being cited as prefixes; (2) by the procedure for neutral esters and acid salts; the components present are cited in the order, cation, hydrocarbyl group, hydrogen,...

records.title: Substitutive nomenclature is senior to functional class nomenclature for preferred IUPAC for esters
records.source_quote: Examples: 4-(acetyloxy)benzoic acid (PIN) 4-hydroxybenzoic acid acetate 5α-cholestane-3β,6α-diyl diacetate 5α-cholestane-3β,6α-diol diacetate

records.title: Esters of acids modified by functional replacement nomenclature
records.source_quote: P-65.6.3.3.7.1 With the exception of retained names, polycarbonic acids and cyanic acid that are described in P-65.6.3.3.7.2, names of esters are all derived from acids modified by functional replacement whose substitutive names are systematically formed,...

records.title: Names of acids modified by functional replacement are used to generate preferred IUPAC names of corresponding esters. Element symbols O, S, etc. and locants are used to designate the location of organyl groups
records.source_quote: Examples: 3-ethyl 1-S-methyl 1-thiodicarbonate (PIN) CH3-S-CS-O-CH3 O,S-dimethyl carbonodithioate (PIN) (CH3)2CH-S-CN propan-2-yl thiocyanate (PIN)

records.title: In the presence of a characteristic group having seniority for citation as suffix, an ester group is indicated by appropriate prefixes in accordance with the type of attachment of the substituent group
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-S-CS-CO-O-CH2-CS-SH {[(methylsulfanyl)(sulfanylidene)acetyl]oxy}ethane(dithoic acid) (PIN) NC-S-CH2-CH2-CO-S-CH2-CH3 S-ethyl 3-(thiocyanato)propanethioate (PIN) HS-CO-O-CS-O-CH2-CH2-COOH 3-({[(sulfanylcarbonyl)oxy]carbonothioyl}oxy)propanoic...

records.title: Pseudoesters
records.source_quote: Compounds having the generic formula R-CO-O-E, where E is not a carbon atom nor an acyl group belong to this class (see P-65.6.3.1.2). Functional class names are constructed in the manner used for esters. P-65.6.3.4.1 When 'E', in R-CO-O-E, is an element...

records.title: When 'E' is an element belonging to Group 16, the pseudoester is named as a pseudoketone (see P-68.4.2.4)
records.source_quote: Example: CH3-CH2-CH2-CO-S-OO-CH3 [1-(methylperoxy)sulfanyl]butan-1-one (PIN) (not S-methylperoxyl butanethioate)

records.title: Cyclic esters
records.source_quote: Compounds that may be considered as derived from hydroxy carboxylic acids or hydroxy sulfonic acids by loss of water intramolecularly are classified as 'lactones' and 'sultones', respectively. For these compounds heterocyclic names are preferred IUPAC...

records.title: Lactones
examples: (PIN) phenanthrene-1,10:9,8-dicarbolactone A lactone, as a pseudoketone, ranks lower in the seniority of classes than an acid or an ester, but higher than an alcohol, amine, or imine. Examples: 8-oxo-7-oxabicyclo[4.2.
(PIN) 2-oxohexahydro-2H-benzooxete-5,6-dicarboxylic acid (a preferred ring fusion name must have a fusion site and at least two rings of five or more members, see P-52.2.4.
records.source_quote: Intramolecular esters of hydroxy carboxylic acids are 'lactones' and are named in three ways. (1) as heterocyclic pseudoketones by adding the suffix 'one', 'dione', 'thione', etc. and the appropriate multiplicative prefixes to the name of the heterocyclic...

records.title: Sultones and sultines are intramolecular esters of hydroxy sulfonic acids and sulfinic acids, respectively, and may be named in three ways:
records.source_quote: (1) as heterocyclic heterones by adding the suffix 'one', 'dione', 'thione', etc. and the appropriate multiplicative prefixes to the name of the heterocyclic parent hydride; (2) by citing the term 'sultone' or 'sultine' denoting the -O-SO2- or -O-SO- group...

records.title: Lactides are cyclic esters derived by multiple esterification between two (or more) molecules of a hydroxy acid; they are named as heterocyclic compounds
records.source_quote: Examples: 1,4-dioxane-2,5-dione (PIN) 6H,12H,18H-tribenzo[b,f,j][1,5,9]trioxacyclododecine-6,12,18-trione (PIN) (not trisalicylide)

records.title: Other cyclic esters derived from different hydroxy acids or from polybasic acids and polyhydroxy compounds are named as heterocycles
records.source_quote: Examples: 1,3-dioxan-2-one (PIN) 1,3,2λ5-dioxaphosphepan-2-one (PIN) 3,4-dihydro-2,5-benzodioxocine-1,6-dione (PIN) (not 3,4-dihydrobenzo[f][1,4]dioxocine-1,6-dione) octahydro[1,4]dioxocino[2,3-c][1,6]dioxecine-2,5,9,12-tetrone (PIN)...

records.title: Acylals
records.source_quote: Acylals are a class of compounds with the general structures R-CH(O-CO-R')2, RR'C(OCOR'')2, etc. Specific compounds are named as esters.

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: Anhydrides are compounds consisting of two acyl groups bonded to the same oxygen atom, i.e., acyl-O-acyl. Symmetric and mixed anhydrides have identical and different acyl groups, respectively. The central oxygen atom can be replaced by chalcogen atoms or a...

records.title: Symmetric anhydrides
records.source_quote: Symmetric anhydrides of monobasic acids, substituted or unsubstituted, are named by replacing the term 'acid' of an acid name by the class name 'anhydride'.

records.title: Mixed anhydrides
records.source_quote: Anhydrides derived from different monobasic acids are named by citing in alphabetical order the names of the two acids, substituted or unsubstituted, without the class name 'acid' followed by the class name 'anhydride' as a separate word.

records.title: Thioanhydrides and other chalcogen analogues
records.source_quote: Chalcogen analogues of anhydrides having the general structure -CO-X-CO-, -CO-X-CS-, or -CS-X-CS-, where X is -S -, -Se -, or -Te-, are named using the class names 'thioanhydride', 'selenoanhydride', or 'telluroanhydride', respectively.

records.title: Peroxyanhydrides and chalcogen analogues
examples: (PIN) Related anhydrides, in which the junction between two acyl groups is of the type -SS-, -OS-, -SSe-, etc. are named as 'dithioperoxyanhydrides', 'thioperoxyanhydrides', 'selenothioperoxyanhydrides', etc. When it is necessary to specify the position of the chalcogen atoms between two unsymmetrically substituted acyl groups or two different acyl groups, the acyl group name is preceded by the appropriate italicized capital element symbols indicating its attachment. Examples:
tables_or_terms: , are named by replacing the term
of an acid or two different acids by the class name
records.source_quote: Peroxyanhydrides, R-CO-OO-CO-R or R-CO-OO-COR', are named by replacing the term 'acid' of an acid or two different acids by the class name 'peroxyanhydride'.

records.title: Anhydrides derived from peroxy acids and their chalcogen analogues are named substitutively as pseudoketones (see P-64.3). Multiplicative names are preferred when the conditions for their use are fulfilled (see P-15.3)
examples: (PIN) {not 1-[(acetylsulfanyl)peroxy]ethan-1-one; the PIN has the lower alphanumerical order}
records.source_quote: Examples: 1,1'-trioxidanediyldi(ethan-1-one) (PIN) 1-(acetyltetrasulfanyl)propan-1-one (PIN) CH3-CO-S-O-S-CO-CH3 1,1'-dithioxanediyldi(ethan-1-one) (PIN) CH3-CO-OO-S-CO-CH3 1-[(acetylperoxy)sulfanyl]ethan-1-one (PIN) {not...

records.title: Diacyl derivatives of multiatomic chalcogen chains are named by skeletal replacement ('a') nomenclature when the conditions for its use are fulfilled (see P-15.4 and P-51.3.1)
records.source_quote: Example: 3-oxa-6-thia-5-selena-4-telluraoctane-2,7-dione (PIN)

records.title: Di- and polyanhydrides
records.source_quote: Di- and polyanhydrides have two or more -CO-O-CO- or related groups, such as -SO2-O-SO2-, respectively. They are named using the class name 'dianhydride', 'trianhydride', etc., preceded by the names of the acid groups cited as separate words. P-65.7.6.1...

records.title: Linear polyanhydrides are named by one of the following methods:
records.source_quote: (1) by selecting the preferred dicarboxylic acid and citing the adjoining acid groups one of which will be substituted using the principles of substitutive nomenclature; (2) by citing the acid groups, in their order of appearance, in the structure...

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of di- and polyanhydrides
records.source_quote: When chalcogen atoms are present in di- and polyanhydrides, their names are formed in different ways.

records.title: When all anhydride linkages are identical, as in -CO-S-CO-, names are formed using class names such as 'thioanhydride' preceded by the multiplicative prefixes 'bis-', 'tris-', etc
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CO-S-CO-CH2-CH2-CO-S-CO-CH3 diacetic butanedioic bis(thioanhydride) (PIN) CH3-CO-S-SO2-CH2-SO2-S-CO-CH3 diacetic methanedisulfonic bis(thioanhydride) (PIN) P-65.7.6.4.2 When different chalcogen atoms are present in the anhydride linkages, the...

records.title: When the chalcogen atom replaces an oxygen atom in a carbonyl group, i.e. >C=S, thiocarboxylic acids and thioacyl groups are used in the manner described for anhydrides and polyanhydrides
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CS-O-CO-CH2-CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3 acetic butanedioic ethanethioic dianhydride (PIN) CH3-CO-O-CS-CH2-CH2-CS-O-CO-CH3 diacetic butanebis(thioic) dianhydride (PIN) CH3-CO-O-CS-CH2-CH2-CS-S-CS-CH3 acetic...

records.title: Cyclic anhydrides formed from two acid groups attached to the same parent hydride structure are named in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) as heterocyclic pseudoketones; (2) by changing the class term 'acid' to 'anhydride' in the systematic or retained name of the dibasic acid. Method (1) generates preferred IUPAC names

records.title: Cyclic dianhydrides formed from four acid groups attached to the same parent hydride structure are named in two ways:
examples: (PIN) [numbering shown in (I)] {not 4,9-dioxatricyclo[4.4.2.02,7]dodecane-3,5,8,10-tetrone [numbering shown in (II)]} cyclohexane-1,2,3,4-tetracarboxylic 1,3:2,4-dianhydride [numbering shown in (III)]
records.source_quote: (1) as heterocyclic pseudoketones; (2) by changing the class term 'acid' to 'dianhydride' in the systematic or retained name of the tetrabasic acid; the locants of the pair of acid groups are separated by a semicolon. Method (1) generates preferred IUPAC names

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of cyclic anhydrides are named:
records.source_quote: (1) as heterocyclic pseudoketones; (2) by changing the class term 'acid' to 'dianhydride' or 'thioanhydride', 'bis(thioanhydride)', etc., in the systematic or retained name of the dibasic or tetrabasic acid. Method (1) leads to the preferred IUPAC name.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: The classes dealt with in this Section have in common the fact that their retained names are derived from those of acids by changing the ‘ic acid’ ending to a class name, for example ‘amide’, ‘ohydrazide’, ‘nitrile’, or ‘aldehyde’.

records.title: Secondary and tertiary amides
records.source_quote: P-66.1.3 ‘Hidden’ amides

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: Amides are derivatives of organic oxoacids in which each hydroxy group has been replaced by an amino or substituted amino group.

records.title: Carboxamides
records.source_quote: Names of carboxamides are formed in two ways:

records.title: Amide names formed by substitutive nomenclature
records.source_quote: P-66.1.1.1.1.1 Alicyclic mono- and diamides are named substitutively by adding the suffix ‘amide’, to the appropriate parent hydride name, with elision of the final letter ‘e’ before ‘a’.

records.title: If an unbranched chain is directly linked to more than two –CO-NH2 groups, these groups are named from the parent hydride by substitutive use of the suffix ‘carboxamide’
records.source_quote: Example: propane-1,2,3-tricarboxamide (PIN)

records.title: The suffix ‘carboxamide’ is always used to name amides with the –CO-NH2 group attached to a ring, ring system, or to a heteroacyclic parent
records.source_quote: Examples: H2P-CO-NH2 phosphanecarboxamide (PIN) H2N-NH-CO-NH2 hydrazinecarboxamide (PIN) thiophene-2-carboxamide (PIN) piperidine-1-carboxamide (PIN)

records.title: Amide names formed by modifying retained names of acids
records.source_quote: Names of amides derived from carboxylic acids listed in P-65.1.1 are formed by changing the ‘ic acid’ or ‘-oic acid’ ending of the retained names of carboxylic acids into ‘amide’.

records.title: Only the following four retained names are preferred IUPAC names and can be substituted. The name ‘oxamide’ is a contracted name from ‘oxalamide’ and substitution on the nitrogen atoms is allowed
records.source_quote: CH3-CO-NH2 acetamide (PIN) C6H5-CO-NH2 benzamide (PIN) H2N-CO-CO-NH2 oxamide (PIN) NC-NH2 cyanamide (PIN, see P-66.1.6.2)

records.title: The traditional name ‘formamide’ is retained for HCO-NH2 and is the preferred IUPAC name. Substitution is permitted on the –NH2 group. Substitution of the aldehydic hydrogen is subject to limitations (see P-65.1.8)
records.source_quote: Examples: HCO-NH2 formamide (PIN) Cl-CO-NH2 carbonochloridic amide (PIN) (not 1-chloroformamide) P-66.1.1.1.2.3 For general nomenclature only, the names furanamide, phthalamide, isophthalamide, and terephthalamide are retained with substitution allowed (see P-65.1.1.2.1).

records.title: Amides derived from retained acid names given in P-65.1.1.2 are only used in general nomenclature; no substitution is allowed, even on amide nitrogen atoms. Preferred IUPAC names are systematic names as given by P-65.1.2
examples: substitution is not allowed on acrylamide)
records.source_quote: Examples: prop-2-enamide (PIN) acrylamide N-methylprop-2-enamide (PIN) (not N-methylacrylamide; substitution is not allowed on acrylamide) 2-hydroxypropanamide (PIN) lactamide 2-hydroxy-N-methylpropanamide (PIN) (not N-methyllactamide)

records.title: Names for amides derived from carbohydrate acids and α-amino acids are discussed in P-102.5.6.6.2.1, P-102.5.6.6.5, and in P-103.2.7, respectively
records.source_quote: Examples: R = –CO-NH2 methyl β-D-galactopyranosiduronamide 2-aminoacetamide glycinamide

records.title: Sulfonamides, sulfinamides, and related selenium and tellurium amides Sulfonamides, sulfinamides, and the analogous selenium and tellurium amides are
records.source_quote: named substitutively using the following suffixes: –SO2-NH2 sulfonamide (preselected suffix) –SO-NH2sulfinamide (preselected suffix) –SeO2-NH2selenonamide (preselected suffix) –SeO-NH2seleninamide (preselected suffix) –TeO2-NH2telluronamide (preselected suffix) –TeO-NH2tellurinamide (preselected suffix) These suffixes may be assigned to any position of a parent hydride.

records.title: N-Substitution
examples: substitution is not allowed on propionamide)
records.source_quote: P-66.1.1.3.1.1 Substituted primary amides, with general structures such as R-CO-NHR′ and R-CO-NR′R′′, and the corresponding amides derived from chalcogen acids are named by citing the substituents R′ and R′′ as prefixes preceded by the locant N when one amide group is present.

records.title: Locants for geminal carboxamide groups
records.source_quote: When geminal carboxamide groups are present, the locants N, N′, etc. are used in association with the numerical locant indicating the position of the groups on a chain or ring.

records.title: Hydroxamic acids
records.source_quote: Hydroxamic acids have the generic structure R-CO-NH-OH and are named as N-hydroxy amides (see P-65.1.3.4).

records.title: Amic acids
records.source_quote: Amic acids are derivatives of dicarboxylic acids having a retained name and when one carboxylic group has been changed to a carboxamide group.

records.title: Anilides
records.source_quote: N-Phenyl derivatives of primary amides are called ‘anilides’ and may be named using the term ‘anilide’ in place of ‘amide’ in systematic or retained names of amides.

records.title: General substitution of amides
records.source_quote: Substitution of amides is expressed by prefixes; numerical, N, and N′ locants are used as required.

records.title: Amides denoted as prefixes
records.source_quote: Two different substituent groups can be derived from amides and expressed as prefixes in the presence of a characteristic group having seniority for citation as suffix:

records.title: In the presence of a characteristic group having priority for citation as suffix or if all carbamoyl groups cannot be included in the suffix, the –CO-NH2 group is named in three different ways:
records.source_quote: (1) by using the two prefixes ‘amino’ and ‘oxo’ to denote such groups on terminal atoms of carbon chains having more than one carbon atom; (2) by using the acyl group name ‘carbamoyl’ (see P-65.2.1.5); (3) by using the prefix ‘aminocarbonyl’.

records.title: Substituents of the types –SO2-NH2, –SO-NH2, and their selenium and tellurium analogues
records.source_quote: In the presence of a characteristic group having priority for citation as suffix, the groups –SO2-NH2, –SO-NH2 and related selenium and tellurium groups are named in two ways corresponding to methods (2) and (3) for the –CO-NH2 group above in P-66.1.1.4.1: (2) by using the acyl group ‘sulfamoyl’ (for sulfonamides only) (see P-65.3.2.3); (3) by using the prefixes ‘amino...sulfonyl’, ‘amino...sulfinyl’, ‘amino...selenonyl’, ‘amino...seleninyl’, ‘amino...telluronyl’, or ‘amino...tellurinyl’.

records.title: Substituents of the types –NH-CO-R and –NH-SO2-R
records.source_quote: When a group having preference for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, the group R-CO-NH–, or R-SO2-NH– (and selenium and tellurium analogues) of an N-substituted amide is named in two ways: (1) substitutively, by using a prefix formed by changing the final letter ‘e’ in the complete name of the amide to ‘o’, thus changing the suffixes ‘amide’ and ‘carboxamide’ into ‘amido’ and ‘carboxamido’, respectively, ‘diamide’ to ‘diamido’ or ‘sulfonamide’ to ‘sulfonamido’, etc.; (2) substitutively, by us

records.title: Substituent groups R-CO-N< and R-CO-N= , or R-SO2-N< and R-SO2-N= (and selenium and tellurium analogues)
records.source_quote: When a group having preference for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, the groups R-CO-N< and R-CO-N=, or R-SO2-N< and R-SO2-N= (and selenium and tellurium analogues) of an N-substituted amide are named by combining acyl group names with those of the appropriate nitrogen substituent groups, azanediyl and imino, respectively.

records.title: Prefixes for the group H2N-CO-CO-NH– are ‘oxamoylamino’ (preferred prefix) or ‘amino(oxo)acetamido’. The preferred prefix for the group H2N-CO-CO-N= is ‘oxamoylimino’
records.source_quote: Example: H2N-CO-CO-N=CH-CH2-COOH 3-(oxamoylimino)propanoic acid (PIN) 3-{[amino(oxo)acetyl]imino}propanoic acid.

records.title: Prefixes derived from oxamide for use in multiplicative nomenclature
records.source_quote: Concatenated preferred prefixes for the groups –HN-CO-CO-NH–, >N-CO-CO-N<, and =N-CO-CO-N= are ‘oxalylbis(azanediyl)’, ‘oxalyldinitrilo’, and ‘oxalylbis(azanylylidene)’, respectively.

records.title: Secondary and tertiary amides
records.source_quote: Amides having general formulas (R-CO)2NH, (R-SO2)2NH, etc., and (R-CO)3N, (R-SO2)3N, etc., respectively, are named as N-acyl derivatives of the senior primary amide or preferred prefix.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of amides
records.source_quote: Chalcogen analogues of amides are named systematically.

records.title: Names are formed by using suffixes modified by functional replacement nomenclature using prefixes and infixes
examples: For a more extended list, see Table 4.4.
records.source_quote: Examples: –(C)S-NH2-thioamide (preferred suffix) –CS-NH2-carbothioamide (preferred suffix) –S(O)(S)-NH2-sulfonothioamide (preselected suffix) –S(S)(S)-NH2-sulfonodithioamide (preselected suffix) –S(S)-NH2-sulfinothioamide (preselected suffix) For a more extended list, see Table 4.4.

records.title: Names for chalcogen analogues of secondary and tertiary amides
records.source_quote: Names are formed by the addition of appropriate prefixes to names described in P-65.1.7.2.3 Examples: CH3-CS-NH-CS-CH3 N-(ethanethioyl)ethanethioamide (PIN) N-(thioacetyl)thioacetamide N-cyclohexyl-N-(ethanethioyl)ethanethioamide (PIN) N-cyclohexyl-N-(thioacetyl)thioacetamide CH3-CH2-CS-NH-CO-CH3 N-(propanethioyl)acetamide (PIN)

records.title: Names of chalcogen derivatives of ‘hidden’ amides
records.source_quote: Names are formed as described in P-64.6.1 for chalcogen derivatives of pseudoketones.

records.title: Names of substituent groups derived from chalcogen derivatives of amides In the presence of a function having priority for citation as suffix, the amide function is expressed in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) by a prefix formed by changing the final letter ‘e’ in the name of the amide into ‘o’; (2) by the appropriate prefixes, such as amino, in conjunction with sulfanylidene or thioxo, as well as carbonothioyl (not thiocarbonyl) for –CS– or carbamothioyl (not thiocarbamoyl) for –CS-NH2.

records.title: Lactams and lactims
records.source_quote: Intramolecular amides of amino carboxylic acids, –CO-NH–, are called ‘lactams’ and their tautomers, –C(OH)=N–, are ‘lactims’.

records.title: Intramolecular amides of amino sulfonic acids are called ‘sultams’ and may be named in three ways
records.source_quote: (1) as heterocyclic heterones; (2) by citing the term ‘sultam’ denoting the cyclic –NH-SO2– group after the name of the appropriate parent hydride preceded by a pair of locants describing the points of attachment of the sulfonyl group and the nitrogen atom, respectively; the locant of the sulfonyl group is cited first, and, if there is a choice, is the lower locant.

records.title: Sultims are tautomers of sultams and are named as described in P-66.1.5.2.1 for sultams, using the term ‘sultim’ in place of ‘sultam’
records.source_quote: Examples: (1) 1-hydroxy-4,5-dihydro-3H-1λ6,2-thiazol-1-one (PIN) (3) 1-hydroxy-4,5-dihydro-3H-1λ4,2-thiazole 1-oxide (2) propane-1,3-sultim (1) 1-hydroxy-1λ6-thia-2-azacyclododec-1-en-1-one (PIN) (3) 1-hydroxy-1λ4-thia-2-azacyclododec-1-ene 1-oxide (2) decane-1,10-sultim

records.title: Intramolecular amides of amino sulfinic acids
records.source_quote: Cyclic amides of amino sulfinic acids and their tautomers are named as heterocyclic compounds.

records.title: Urea and its substitutive derivatives
records.source_quote: P-66.1.6.1.1.1 The compound H2N-CO-NH2 has the retained name ‘urea’, which is the preferred IUPAC name, with locants N and N′, as shown above the structure below.

records.title: The prefixes for appropriate substituent groups derived from urea are formed systematically. The prefixes ‘ureido’ and ‘ureylene’ are not recommended
records.source_quote: The prefixes ‘ureido’ and ‘ureylene’ are no longer acceptable in IUPAC nomenclature.

records.title: Carboxylic acid derivatives of urea
records.source_quote: Two carboxylic acids are related to urea; have been known as ‘allophanic acid’ for H2N-CO-NH-COOH and ‘hydantoic acid’ for H2N-CO-NH-CH2-COOH.

records.title: Seniority order of urea among amides
examples: formamide is preferred to urea, see P-41]
records.source_quote: Amides are ranked in the same way as the corresponding acids (see P-42).

records.title: Isourea and its derivatives
records.source_quote: P-66.1.6.1.2.1 The imidic acid tautomer of urea, H2N-C(OH)=NH, is named ‘carbamimidic acid’, a shortened form of the systematic functional replacement name ‘carbonamidimidic acid’.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of urea and isourea
records.source_quote: P-66.1.6.1.3.1 Chalcogen analogues of urea are named by functional replacement nomenclature using the prefixes ‘thio’, ‘seleno’, and ‘telluro’.

records.title: Prefixes for substituents derived from chalcogen analogues of urea and isourea are as follows:
records.source_quote: H2N-CS-NH–carbamothioylamino (preferred prefix) [amino(sulfanylidene)methyl]amino- HN=C(SH)-NH–(C-sulfanylcarbonimidoyl)amino (preferred prefix) [imino(sulfanyl)methyl]amino- H2N-C(SH)=N–[amino(sulfanyl)methylidene]amino (preferred prefix) Example: 3-(carbamothioylamino)propanoic acid (PIN) 3-{[amino(sulfanylidene)methyl]amino}propanoic acid

records.title: Condensed ureas
examples: For polyurets, where n = 5 and higher, skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature leads to preferred IUPAC names.
records.source_quote: Condensed ureas, H2N-[CO-NH]n-H, where n = 2, 3, or 4, are named systematically as diamides of imidodicarbonic acid, diimidotricarbonic acid, and triimidotetracarbonic acid, etc., functional replacement names derived from the corresponding di- or polycarbonic acids.

records.title: Amides derived from cyanic acid
records.source_quote: The traditional name ‘cyanamide’ is retained for NC-NH2 and is the preferred IUPAC name.

records.title: Amides of di- and polycarbonic acids
records.source_quote: Systematic names for amides of the polycarbonic acids are formed by adding the functional class name ‘amide’ to that of the corresponding acid, preceded by the numerical prefix ‘di’ to indicate the presence of two –NH2 groups.

records.title: Polyfunctional amides
records.source_quote: Amides follow acids, anhydrides, esters, and acid halides in the seniority order of compound classes expressed by suffixes (see P-41); within the amide class, amides rank in the same order as the corresponding acid.

records.title: IMIDES
examples: substitution is not allowed on succinimide)
records.source_quote: P-66.2.1 Imides are compounds containing the structural grouping –CO-NH-CO–.

records.title: Definition
records.source_quote: Hydrazides are compounds derived from the organic oxoacids denoted by a suffix, such as –COOH, –SO2-OH, –SO-OH, etc., by replacing –OH groups with –NH-NH2 groups.

records.title: Systematic names
records.source_quote: Hydrazides of the type R-CO-NH-NH2 are named in two ways: (1) by substitutive nomenclature; (2) by modification of retained names of carboxylic acids.

records.title: Substitutive nomenclature

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-16.9, P-66.3.1.2, P-2, P-15, P-13, P-14

records.source_quote: Hydrazides are named substitutively by using the following suffixes (see Table 4.4).

records.title: Names formed by modifying retained names of carboxylic acids
records.source_quote: Names of hydrazides are formed by changing the ‘ic acid’ or ‘-oic acid’ ending of the retained names of carboxylic acids into ‘ohydrazide’ (for nitrogen locants, see P-66.3.3).

records.title: Hydrazides derived from carbohydrate acids and α-amino acids are discussed in P-102.5.6.6.2.1 and P-103.2.7, respectively
records.source_quote: Examples: D-gluconohydrazide 2-aminoacetohydrazide glycinohydrazide

records.title: Substituent groups derived from hydrazides
records.source_quote: Substituent groups corresponding to hydrazides are of two types: –CO-NH-NH2 , –SO2-NH-NH2, etc.; and –NH-NH-CO-R , –NH-NH-SO2-R , etc.

records.title: Substituent groups of the type –CO-NH-NH2, –SO2-NH-NH2, etc., may be named in three ways except when the –CO-NH-NH2 group is at the end of a carbon chain:
records.source_quote: (1) as an acyl group derived from the corresponding acid, which gives the preferred prefix; or (2) as the appropriate carbonohydrazidoyl acyl prefix; or (3) by concatenation using the prefix hydrazinyl with carbonyl.

records.title: When the –CO-NHNH2 group is at the end of a chain the use of the prefixes ‘hydrazinyl’ and ‘oxo’ generates preferred IUPAC names
records.source_quote: Example: 3-hydrazinyl-3-oxopropanoic acid (PIN) (hydrazinecarbonyl)acetic acid carbonohydrazidoylacetic acid

records.title: When a group having priority for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, a hydrazide group of the type R-CO-NH-NH– or R-SO2-NH-NH– (or the analogous selenium or tellurium group) is named:
examples: The groups R-CO-N(NH2)–, R-SO2N(NH2)–, etc. are named as substituted derivatives of the group ‘hydrazinyl,’ with numerical locants. This method leads to preferred prefixes.
records.source_quote: (1) by expressing the corresponding hydrazide as a prefix by replacing the final letter ‘e’ in the name of the hydrazide by the letter ‘o’, for example, ‘acetohydrazido’, ‘propanehydrazido’, and ‘benzohydrazido’; the locant N designates the nitrogen atom adjacent to the –CO– group.

records.title: Substituted hydrazides
records.source_quote: P-66.3.3.1 Alkyl, aryl, cycloalkyl, etc. substituents on the nitrogen atoms of hydrazides are described by the appropriate prefix names and the locants ‘N’ for –NH– and ‘N′’ for –NH2, as illustrated below.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of hydrazides
records.source_quote: Chalcogen analogues of hydrazides are named substitutively using suffixes formed by functional replacement, i.e., ‘thiohydrazide’, ‘carbothiohydrazide’, ‘sulfonothiohydrazide’, etc., as described in P-33.2.2 and Table 4.4.

records.title: Preferred IUPAC names for hydrazides derived from carbonic and cyanic acids are chosen according to the seniority order of classes
examples: [cyanic acid is a retained name (see P-65.2.2)]
records.source_quote: Examples: cyanohydrazide (PIN) [cyanic acid is a retained name (see P-65.2.2)] carbononitridic hydrazide hydrazinecarbonitrile hydrazinecarboxylic acid (PIN) carbonohydrazidic acid hydrazinecarbohydrazide (PIN) carbonic dihydrazide P-66.3.5.2 Names of hydrazides derived from the di- and polycarbonic acids are formed by adding the functional class name ‘hydrazide’ to that of the corresponding acid, preceded by the numerical prefix ‘di’ to express multiplicity of hydrazide groups, when necessary.

records.title: Corresponding substituent groups
records.source_quote: When a group having priority for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, the hydrazide group is named: (1) as an acylhydrazinyl compound; the hydrazinyl group is numbered by the numerical locants 1 and 2; (2) by expressing the corresponding hydrazide substitutively as a prefix by replacing the final letter ‘e’ in the name of the hydrazide by the letter ‘o’.

records.title: Semioxamazones
records.source_quote: Semioxamazones have the general structure R=N-NH-CO-CO-NH2.

records.title: Amidines
records.source_quote: Compounds having the general structure R-C(=NH)-NH2 generically are known as ‘carboxamidines’ and those having the general structure R-S(=NH)-NH2 as ‘sulfinamidines’.

records.title: Suffixes for amidines
records.source_quote: Amidines are named as amides by functional replacement nomenclature in which the =O atom has been replaced by the =NH group.

records.title: Guanidine and its derivatives
records.source_quote: P-66.4.1.2.1.1 The preferred IUPAC name for the ‘amidine’ related to carbonic acid, H2N-C(=NH)-NH2, is the retained name ‘guanidine’; the locants N, N′ and N′′ are used in preferred IUPAC names.

records.title: Hydrocarbyl derivatives are named as substituted guanidines. When the position of the double bond is unknown, the preferred IUPAC name uses a minimum number of primes
records.source_quote: Examples: N,N,N′,N′-tetramethyl-N′′-phenylguanidine (PIN) N,N,N′,N′-tetramethyl-N′′-phenylcarbonimidic diamide N,N,N′-trimethylguanidine (PIN) N,N,N′-trimethylcarbonimidic diamide or N,N′-dimethylguanidine (PIN) (not N,N′′-dimethylguanidine) N,N′-dimethylcarbonimidic diamide

records.title: In the presence of a characteristic group having seniority over guanidine (see item 11 in P-41), the following prefixes are used. The prefix guanidino may be used in general nomenclature
records.source_quote: carbamimidoylamino (preferred prefix) carbamimidamido [amino(imino)methyl]amino guanidino (H2N)2C=N– (diaminomethylidene)amino (preferred prefix) Examples: (H2N)2C=N-CH2-COOH N-(diaminomethylidene)glycine [(diaminomethylidene)amino]acetic acid 4-[(diaminomethylidene)amino]butanoic acid (PIN) 4-[methyl(N-methyl-N-phenylcarbamimidoyl)amino]benzoic acid (PIN) 4-({imino[methyl(phenyl)amino]methyl}methylamino)benzoic acid 4-(N,N′-dimethyl-N′-phenylcarbamimidamido)benzoic acid H2N-C(=NH)-NH-CHO N-carbamimidoylformamide (

records.title: Condensed guanidines
examples: For polyguanides, where n = 5 and higher, skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature leads to preferred IUPAC names
records.source_quote: The names biguanide, triguanide, etc., are no longer recommended.

records.title: Prefixes for the amidine characteristic group
records.source_quote: P-66.4.1.3.1 The systematic name for the group –C(=NH)-NH2 is ‘carbamimidoyl’; it is the name of the acyl group derived from the name carbamimidic acid, HO-C(=NH)-NH2 and is the preferred IUPAC prefix.

records.title: When the carbon atom of the H2N-C(=NH)– group terminates a chain, the groups –NH2 and =NH are designated by the prefixes ‘amino’ and ‘imino’, respectively
records.source_quote: Example: methyl 4-(dimethylamino)-4-(ethylimino)butanoate (PIN) (not methyl 3-[C-(dimethylamino)-N-ethylcarbonimidoyl]propanoate) (not methyl 3-(N2-ethyl-N1,N1-dimethylamidino)propionate) P-66.4.1.3.3 The substituent group HN=CH-NH– is named ‘methanimidamido’; it can also be named as a compound substituent prefix ‘formimidoylamino’ or a complex substituent prefix ‘(iminomethyl)amino’. ‘Methanimidamido’ is the preferred IUPAC prefix.

records.title: In the presence of a characteristic group cited as a suffix, the group R-C(=NH)NH– or R-S(O)(=NH)NH– and their selenium and tellurium analogues are named in two ways
records.source_quote: (1) substitutively, by using a prefix formed by changing the final letter ‘e’ in the complete name of the amide to ‘o’, thus changing the suffixes ‘imidamide’ and ‘carboximidamide’ into ‘imidamido’ and ‘carboximidamido’, respectively, or ‘sulfonimidamide’ to ‘sulfonimidamido’, etc.

records.title: Substituted amidines
records.source_quote: P-66.4.1.4.1 N-Substituted amidines are named by prefixing the name of the appropriate substituent to the name of the unsubstituted imidamides, with N and N′ as locants when there is only one imidamide suffix; the locant N refers to the amino group and N′ refers to the imino group.

records.title: Geminal carboxamidine groups
records.source_quote: When geminal carboxamidine groups are present, the locants N, N′, N′′, N′′′ are used.

records.title: Formamidine disulfides
examples: Note: The anhydride is the higher class in seniority (see P-41).
records.source_quote: The compound H2N-C(=NH)-S-S-C(=NH)-NH2 and its derivatives have been named previously on the basis of the parent structure ‘formamidine disulfide’.

records.title: Diamidides
records.source_quote: Diamidides are analogues of acyclic carboxylic anhydrides in which the =O atoms have been replaced by =NR groups and the anhydride oxygen atom by –NR– giving the general formula R-C(=NR′)-N(R′′)-C(=NR′′′)-R′′′ ′.

records.title: Amidrazone suffixes
records.source_quote: Compounds having the general structure R-C(NH2)=N-NH2 or the tautomeric structure R-C(=NH)-NH-NH2 have the class name ‘amidrazones’ and are named substitutively using the suffixes ‘hydrazonamide’ or ‘carbohydrazonamide’, and ‘imidohydrazide’ or ‘carboximidohydrazide’, respectively.

records.title: Amidrazones of carbonic acid and di- and polycarbonic acids
records.source_quote: The general methodology discussed in P-65.2 is applied to generate the names for amidrazones derived from carbonic, di-, and polycarbonic acids.

records.title: Amidrazone prefixes
records.source_quote: P-66.4.2.3.1 Prefixes for the group –C(=NH)-NHNH2 are ‘hydrazinecarboximidoyl’ (preferred prefix) derived from hydrazinecarboximidic acid, and ‘carbonohydrazidimidoyl’, derived from carbonohydrazidimidic acid.

records.title: The preferred prefix for the group H2N-CH=N-NH– is ‘(aminomethylidene)hydrazinyl’. Prefixes for the group HN=CH-NH-NH– are
records.source_quote: ‘methanimidohydrazido’ (preferred prefix) and ‘2-(methanimidoyl)hydrazin-1-yl’ and ‘2-(iminomethyl)hydrazin-1-yl’.

records.title: When a group having preference for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, the groups R-C(=N-NH2)-NH– and R-S(O)(=N-NH2)-NH– (and the selenium and tellurium analogues) are named in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) substitutively, by using a prefix formed by changing the final letter ‘e’ in the complete name of the amide to ‘o’; (2) substitutively, by using ‘acylamino’ prefixes formed by substituting the name of the acyl group to the substituent ‘amino’ Method (1) generates preferred prefixes.

records.title: When a group having priority for citation as a principal characteristic group is present, a hydrazide group of the type R-C(=NH)-NHNH– or R-S(=NH)2-NHNH– (or the analogous selenium and tellurium group) is named:
records.source_quote: (1) by expressing the corresponding hydrazide as a prefix by replacing the final letter ‘e’ in the name of the hydrazide by the letter ‘o’: (2) as an acylhydrazinyl prefix; the hydrazinyl group is numbered by using the numerical locants 1 and 2.

records.title: Hydrazidine suffixes
examples: (for locants N, N′, etc. in association with numerical locants,
records.source_quote: Compounds with the general structure R-C(=N-NH2)-NH-NH2 have the class name ‘hydrazidines’ and are named substitutively by using the suffixes ‘hydrazonohydrazide’ and ‘carbohydrazonohydrazide’ as prescribed for hydrazides.

records.title: Hydrazidines derived from sulfonic and sulfinic acids, and similar selenium and tellurium acids, are named in accordance with the same principles
records.source_quote: Example: benzenesulfinohydrazonohydrazide (PIN)

records.title: Hydrazidines derived from carbonic acid and di- and polycarbonic acids follow the procedure for the corresponding hydrazides given above (see P-66.3.5)
records.source_quote: Examples: hydrazinecarbohydrazonohydrazide (PIN) carbonohydrazonic dihydrazide (P-65.2.1.3) dicarbonohydrazonic dihydrazide (PIN, P-65.2.1.3) (not dihydrazonodicarbonic dihydrazide)

records.title: Hydrazidine prefixes
records.source_quote: P-66.4.3.4.1 In the presence of a senior characteristic group, the prefixes for the group –C(=N-NH2)-NH-NH2 are ‘hydrazinecarbohydrazonoyl’ (preferred prefix) or ‘C-hydrazinylcarbonohydrazonoyl’.

records.title: Amidoximes (amide oximes)
records.source_quote: Amide oximes are formally oximes of carboxamides, i.e., compounds having the general structure R-C(=N-OH)-NH2 and derivatives formed by substitution.

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: Compounds with the general structure R-C≡N are called ‘nitriles’ or ‘cyanides’.

records.title: Nomenclature for generating preferred names of nitriles
records.source_quote: Compounds of the general structure R-C≡N have the class names ‘nitriles’ and are named in three ways: (1) substitutively, using the suffixes ‘nitrile’ for –(C)N and ‘carbonitrile for –CN; (2) by changing the ‘ic acid’ or ‘oic acid’ endings in retained names of carboxylic acids into ‘onitrile’; the nomenclatural properties of acids are transferred to nitriles; thus, preferred names of nitriles correspond to preferred names of carboxylic acids (see P-65.1.1.1) and carboxylic acids that are not substitutable generate

records.title: Acyclic mono- and dinitriles are named in the following two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) substitutively by using the suffix ‘nitrile’; and (2) by functional class nomenclature using the class name ‘cyanide’.

records.title: The suffix ‘carbonitrile’ is always used to name nitriles having the –CN group attached to a ring or ring system or to an acyclic heteroatom
records.source_quote: Examples: H3Si-CN silanecarbonitrile (PIN) silyl cyanide H2N-NH-CN hydrazinecarbonitrile cyanohydrazide (PIN; see P-66.3.1.2.1) hydrazinyl cyanide (see P-66.5.1.3) cyclohexanecarbonitrile (PIN) cyclohexyl cyanide piperidine-1-carbonitrile (PIN) (benzo[1,2:4,5]di[7]annulene)-2-carbonitrile (PIN) (benzo[1,2:4,5]dicycloheptene)-2-carbonitrile P-66.5.1.1.4 When a group is present that has priority for citation as the principal characteristic group or when all –CN groups cannot be expressed as the principal characterist

records.title: Nitriles derived from retained names of carboxylic acids

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-66.5.1.2.1, P-65.1.8, P-66.5.1.2.2, P-65.1.1.2.1, P-65.1.2, P-65.1.1

records.source_quote: P-66.5.1.2.1 The following names are preferred IUPAC names, with unlimited substitution, except for formonitrile whose substitution rules are the same as formic acid (see P-65.1.8) and, obviously, for oxalonitrile for which no substitution is possible.

records.title: Nitriles derived from retained acid names given in P-65.1.1.2.2 are only used in general nomenclature; no substitution is allowed. Preferred IUPAC names are systematic names (see P-66.5.1.1)
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CH2-CN propiononitrile propanenitrile (PIN) NC-CH2-CH2-CN succinonitrile butanedinitrile (PIN)

records.title: Names of nitriles derived from carbohydrate acids and amino acids are discussed in P-102.5.6.6.2.1 and P-103.2.8, respectively
records.source_quote: Examples: L-xylononitrile (see P-102.5.6.6.2.1) H2N-CH2-CN aminoacetonitrile (see P-103.2.8) glycinonitrile

records.title: Functional class nomenclature for generating preferred IUPAC names of cyanides
records.source_quote: Functional class nomenclature is used, when needed, to name compounds in accordance with the seniority of classes and to name compounds that cannot be named substitutively, for example, cyanides corresponding to sulfonic acid, sulfinic acids and their selenium and tellurium analogues, carbonic acid, cyanic acid, and inorganic acids.

records.title: Nitriles with an α-oxo group
records.source_quote: Compounds of the type R-CO-CN can be named as acyl cyanides in a way similar to acid halides.

records.title: Substituted nitriles

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-65.1.2.3, P-65.1.2.4, P-65.1.1.2, P-41, P-2, P-14

examples: no substitution for this retained name, see P-65.1.1.2)
no substitution on propionitrile, see P-65.1.1.2)
records.source_quote: Substituents on the parent hydrides are denoted as prefixes.

records.title: Nitriles corresponding to carbonic acid and di- and polycarbonic acids are named by functional class nomenclature
records.source_quote: Examples: NC-CO-CN carbonyl dicyanide (PIN) 2-oxopropanedinitrile (not oxomalononitrile; no substitution allowed on malononitrile) NC-CO-CO-CN oxalyl dicyanide (PIN) 2,3-dioxobutanedinitrile NC-C(=NH)-CN carbonimidoyl dicyanide (PIN) (not 2-iminopropanedinitrile) NC-C(=NNH2)-CN carbonohydrazonoyl dicyanide (PIN) (not 2-hydrazonopropanedinitrile) H2N-CO-CN carbamoyl cyanide (PIN) NC-CO-O-CO-CN dicarbonic dicyanide (PIN) (see P-65.5.3.2)

records.title: Nitrile oxides and chalcogen analogues
records.source_quote: P-66.5.4.1 Compounds with the general structure R-C≡NO have the generic name ‘nitrile oxides’.

records.title: When it is necessary to cite the group –C≡NO as a substituent prefix, method (2), above is applied
records.source_quote: Examples: 4-(methoxycarbonyl)benzonitrile oxide (PIN) (not methyl 4-[(oxo-λ5-azanylidyne)methyl]benzoate) (no longer methyl 4-isofulminatobenzoate) sodium 4-[(oxo-λ5-azanylidyne)methyl]benzoate (PIN) (no longer sodium 4-isofulminatobenzoate)

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: The class name ‘aldehyde’ traditionally refers to compounds containing the –CH=O group attached to a carbon atom.

records.title: Systematic names of aldehydes
records.source_quote: Aldehydes are systematically named in three ways: (1) substitutively, using the suffixes ‘al’ for –(C)HO and ‘carbaldehyde’ for –CHO; (2) by changing the ‘ic acid’ or ‘oic acid’ endings of retained names of carboxylic acids into ‘aldehyde’; the nomenclatural properties of acids are transferred to aldehydes; thus, preferred names of aldehydes correspond to preferred names of acids, and carboxylic acids that are not substitutable generate nonsubstitutable aldehydes; (3) by using the prefixes ‘oxo’, denoting =O, or ‘f

records.title: Mono- and dialdehydes derived from alkanes are named substitutively using the suffix ‘al’ added to the name of the parent hydride with elision of the final letter ‘e’ of the parent hydride before ‘a’
records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CH2-CH2-CH2-CHO pentanal (PIN) OHC-CH2-CH2-CH2-CHO pentanedial (PIN)

records.title: The suffix ‘carbaldehyde’ is used when more than two –CHO groups are attached to an unbranched alkane chain
records.source_quote: Example: butane-1,2,4-tricarbaldehyde (PIN)

records.title: The suffix ‘carbaldehyde’ is used when the –CHO group is attached to a carbon atom of a ring or ring system, or to a heteroatom
examples: hydrazide is senior to an aldehyde)
records.source_quote: Examples: cyclohexanecarbaldehyde (PIN) pyridine-2,6-dicarbaldehyde (PIN) H2P-CHO phosphanecarbaldehyde (PIN) H2NNH-CHO hydrazinecarbaldehyde formohydrazide (PIN; hydrazide is senior to an aldehyde) 1,4,4-trimethyltetraaz-2-ene-1-carbaldehyde (PIN)

records.title: Names of aldehydes derived from retained names of carboxylic acids
records.source_quote: Names of aldehydes derived from retained names are formed by changing the ‘ic acid’ or ‘oic acid’ ending of the retained names of carboxylic acids to ‘aldehyde’.

records.title: The following names are preferred IUPAC names, with substitution allowed for acetaldehyde and benzaldehyde. Substitution rules for formaldehyde are the same as for formic acid (see P-65.1.8)
records.source_quote: HCHO formaldehyde (PIN) methanal CH3-CHO acetaldehyde (PIN) ethanal C6H5-CHO benzaldehyde (PIN) benzenecarbaldehyde P-66.6.1.2.2 For general nomenclature, only the names furaldehyde, phthalaldehyde, isophthalaldehyde, and terephthalaldehyde are retained with substitution allowed (see P-34).

records.title: Aldehydes derived from retained acid names given in P-65.1.1.2 are only used in general nomenclature; no substitution is allowed. Preferred IUPAC names are systematic names (see P-66.6.1.1)

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-65.1.1.2, P-66.6.1.1, P-66.6.1.3, P-44.1.2.2, P-41, P-65.1.1, P-65.1.2

records.source_quote: Examples: CH3-CH2-CHO propionaldehyde propanal (PIN) OHC-CH2-CH2-CHO succinaldehyde butanedial (PIN) P-66.6.1.3 In the presence of a characteristic group having priority to be cited as a suffix or when present on a side chain, a –CHO group is expressed by the preferred prefix ‘oxo’ if located at an end of a carbon chain, or, otherwise, by the preferred prefix ‘formyl’.

records.title: Aldehydes from di- and polycarbonic acids
records.source_quote: Aldehydes from di- and polycarbonic acids are named on the basis of the higher compound class.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of aldehydes
records.source_quote: Chalcogen analogues of aldehydes are named by using the suffixes and prefixes in Table 6.4.

records.title: Polyfunctional aldehydes
examples: the longest chain is the principal chain (see P-44.3)]
records.source_quote: In the presence of an aldehyde group, ketones, pseudoketones, heterones, hydroxy compounds, amines, and imines are expressed by prefixes.

records.title: Acetals and ketals
records.source_quote: P-66.6.5.1.1 Compounds with the general structure RR′C(O-R′′)(O-R′′′), where only R and R′ may be, but need not be, hydrogen, have the class name ‘acetal’. ‘Ketals’ constitute a subclass of acetals wherein neither R nor R′ may be hydrogen.

records.title: Cyclic acetals and ketals
records.source_quote: Cyclic acetals as the principal function are named as heterocyclic compounds and these names are preferred IUPAC names; cyclic ketals are spiro compounds that are named in accordance with the rules described in Section P-24 giving preferred IUPAC names.

records.title: Hemiacetals and hemiketals
records.source_quote: Compounds with the general structure RR′C(OH)(O-R′′) have the class name ‘hemiacetals’.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of acetals and ketals
records.source_quote: Sulfur analogues of acetals and ketals with the general structures RR′C(S-R′′)(S-R′′′), or RR′C(S-R′′)(O-R′′′), have the class names ‘dithioacetals’ or ‘monothioacetals’, respectively.

records.title: Chalcogen analogues of hemiacetals and hemiketals
records.source_quote: Sulfur analogues of hemiacetals and hemiketals with the general structures RR′C(SH)(S-R′′), RR′C(OH)(S-R′′) or RR′C(SH)(O-R′′), have the class names ‘dithiohemiacetals’ or ‘monothiohemiacetals’, respectively.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: Mono-, di-, and polynuclear noncarbon oxoacids and their chalcogen analogues having retained names are used as parent structures to generate names of carbon containing compounds.

records.title: MONONUCLEAR NONCARBON OXOACIDS

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-12.2, IR-8, P-42, P-41, P-15, P-13.2, P-65

records.source_quote: Retained names for mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids have the following elements as central atoms: N, P, As, Sb, Si, B, S, Se, Te, F, Cl, Br, and I.

records.title: Seniority order among noncarbon oxoacids and derivatives

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-67.1.6, P-41, P-42, P-62.5, P-67.1.4.1, P-67.1.4.1.1.2, P-67.1.5.1, P-67.1.5.2, P-65

records.source_quote: P-67.1.6 aci-Nitro compounds

records.title: Names of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: Preselected names (see P-12.2) of the mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids used for deriving preferred IUPAC names for organic compounds and names for general organic nomenclature are noted in the following list, given in alphabetical order.

records.title: Substitution of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids with hydrogen atoms attached to the central atom (substitutable hydrogen)
records.source_quote: Acids with hydrogen atoms attached to the central atom may be substituted by organyl groups and preferred IUPAC names are formed in this manner.

records.title: Functional replacement nomenclature applied to mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: Mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids are modified by either infixes or prefixes in functional replacement nomenclature.

records.title: Mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids modified by infixes. The following acids are modified by infixes; they are listed in group order B, N, P, As, Sb, S, Se, Te:
records.source_quote: B(OH)3 boric acid HB(OH)2boronic acid H2B(OH)borinic acid N(O)(OH)3nitroric acid (hypothetical) N(OH)3azorous acid (hypothetical) HN(O)(OH)2 azonic acid H2N(O)(OH)azinic acid HN(OH)2azonous acid P(O)(OH)3phosphoric acid P(OH)3phosphorous acid HP(O)(OH)2phosphonic acid HP(OH)2phosphonous acid H2P(O)(OH)phosphinic acid H2P(OH)phosphinous acid As(O)(OH)3arsoric acid (formerly arsenic acid) As(OH)3arsorous acid (formerly arsen(i)ous acid) HAs(O)(OH)2arsonic acid HAs(OH)2arsonous acid H2As(O)(OH)arsinic acid H2As(OH)ars

records.title: Mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids modified by prefixes. The following acids are modified by prefixes; they are listed in the order Si, N, F, Cl, Br, I
records.source_quote: Si(OH)4 silicic acid (formerly orthosilicic acid) HO-NO2nitric acid HO-NOnitrous acid F(O)3(OH)perfluoric acid F(O)2(OH)fluoric acid F(O)(OH)fluorous acid F(OH)hypofluorous acid Cl(O)3(OH)perchloric acid Cl(O)2(OH)chloric acid Cl(O)(OH)chlorous acid Cl(OH)hypochlorous acid Br(O)3(OH)perbromic acid Br(O)2(OH)bromic acid Br(O)(OH)bromous acid Br(OH)hypobromous acid I(O)3(OH)periodic acid I(O)2(OH)iodic acid I(O)(OH)iodous acid I(OH) hypoiodous acid

records.title: General methodology for functional replacement nomenclature using infixes

exceptions.effect: resolve_dependencies_before_executing_this_rule: P-15.5, P-67.1.2.1, P-67.1.2.2, P-67.1.4.1.1.6, P-67.3.1, P-13.2, P-65

records.source_quote: Functional replacement nomenclature (see P-15.5) using infixes generates functional class names for the following classes: acid halides and pseudo halides (azides, cyanides, isocyanides, and isocyanates), amides, hydrazides, and also imidic, hydrazonic and nitridic acids.

records.title: The following infixes are used to describe the replacement of =O and –OH by chalcogen analogues (in decreasing order of seniority):
records.source_quote: (1)–OO–peroxo (2)–OS– or –SO–thioperoxo (similarly selenoperoxo, telluroperoxo) (3)–SS–dithioperoxo (similarly diselenoperoxo, ditelluroperoxo) (4)–SSe– or –SeS–selenothioperoxo (similarly for other mixed chalcogens) (5)–S– or =Sthio (6)–Se– or =Seseleno (7)–Te– or =Tetelluro

records.title: Infixes denoting replacement of -OH group in decreasing order of seniority with all halides (class 1) and pseudohalides (class 2) having the same rank within the class
records.source_quote: (1)–Brbromido –Clchlorido –Ffluorido –Iiodido (2)–N3azido –OCNcyanatido –CNcyanido –NCOisocyanatido –NCisocyanido –NCSeisoselenocyanatido –NCTeisotellurocyanatido –NCSisothiocyanatido –SeCNselenocyanatido –TeCNtellurocyanatido –SCNthiocyanatidoo (3)–NH2amido (4)–NH-NH2hydrazido (5)≡Nnitrido (6)=NHimido (7)=NNH2hydrazono

records.title: Prefixes denoting chalcogen analogues of acids by replacing oxygen atoms (in decreasing order of seniority):
records.source_quote: (1)–OO–peroxo (2)–OS– or –SO–thioperoxy (similarly selenoperoxy, telluroperoxy) (3)–SS–dithioperoxy (similarly diselenoperoxy, ditelluroperoxy) (4)–SSe– or –SeS–selenothioperoxy (similarly for other mixed chalcogens) (5)–S– or =Sthio (6)–Se– or =Seseleno (7)–Te– or =Tetelluro

records.title: Prefixes denoting replacement of -OH group in decreasing order of seniority with all halides (class 1) and pseudohalides (class 2)
records.source_quote: having the same rank within the class.

records.title: Mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids modified by functional replacement nomenclature
records.source_quote: Preselected names described in P-67.1.2 are used for deriving preferred IUPAC names for organic compounds.

records.title: Examples of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids modified by infixes:
records.source_quote: CH3-B(OH)(SH) methylboronothioic acid (PIN) CH3-B(NH-CH3)(OH) B,N-dimethylboronamidic acid (PIN) CH3-N(OH)(SH) methylazonothious acid (PIN) (C2H5)2P(S)(SH) diethylphosphinodithioic acid (PIN) (CH3)2N-P(O)(OH)2 dimethylphosphoramidic acid (PIN) (C6H5)2P(=N-CH3)(OH) N-methyl-P,P-diphenylphosphinimidic acid (PIN) C6H5-P(=N-C6H5)(Cl)(SH) N,P-diphenylphosphonochloridimidothioic acid (PIN) C6H5-P(S)(NH-CH3)(OH) N-methyl-P-phenylphosphonamidothioic O-acid (PIN) (CH3)2N-P(O)(NCS)(SH) N,N-dimethylphosphoramid(isothiocyanati

records.title: Examples of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids modified by prefixes:
records.source_quote: Si(OH)3(SH) thiosilicic acid (name derived from the preselected name silicic acid) S=N-OH thionitrous O-acid (name derived from the preselected name nitrous acid) Cl(S)2-OH dithiochloric O-acid (name derived from the preselected name chloric acid)

records.title: Specific use of the prefix ‘cyanato’ and the infix ‘cyanatido’
records.source_quote: When attached to the central atom of a mononuclear noncarbon oxoacid, the group –OCN creates an anhydride linkage (see P-67.1.3.3).

records.title: Name construction guidelines for functional replacement nomenclature
records.source_quote: The names phosphonous, phosphinous, phosphonic and phosphinic acid (and similarly for arsenic, antimony and nitrogen acids) can only be used when P, As or Sb is attached to atoms of hydrogen, carbon or another atom of a parent hydride such as N, As, Si.

records.title: Acid halides and pseudohalides
records.source_quote: P-67.1.2.5.1 Except for the boron acids and silicic acid, preferred IUPAC names of acid halides and pseudohalides are formed by adding the class name(s) of a halide or pseudohalide to that of the acid.

records.title: Preferred IUPAC names of halides of the boron acids and silicic acid are substitutive names
records.source_quote: Examples: C6H5-B(Cl)(Br) bromo(chloro)(phenyl)borane (PIN) phenylboronic bromide chloride phenylboronobromidic chloride CH3-SiCl3 trichloro(methyl)silane (PIN) SiCl4 tetrachlorosilane (preselected name) silicon tetrachloride (not silicic tetrachloride)

records.title: Amides and hydrazides
records.source_quote: Amides and hydrazides are named by functional class nomenclature by replacing the term ‘acid’ in the name of the corresponding acid by ‘amide’ or ‘hydrazide’.

records.title: Preferred IUPAC names of amides and hydrazides are denoted by the class name ‘amide’ or ‘hydrazide’:
records.source_quote: (a) when all –OH groups in the corresponding acid have been replaced by –NH2 or –NH-NH2 groups, and (b) when the amide or hydrazide is the principal functional group in accordance with the following order of seniority: Br, Cl, F, I, N3, CN, NC, NCO, ONC, NH2 (amide), (hydrazide) Note: This order is not quite the same as that used by CAS (where amide follows the halogens and precedes the pseudohalogens) but it is consistent with the order of compound classes in P-41.

records.title: Preselected names of amides and hydrazides of the boron acids and silicic acid are substitutive names based on the preselected parent hydride names borane and silane
examples: (name derived from the preselected name borane)
(name derived from the preselected name borane)
(name derived from the preselected name borane)
(name derived from the preselected name silane)
records.source_quote: Examples: H2B-NH2 boranamine (name derived from the preselected name borane) (not borinic amide) B(NH2)3 boranetriamine (name derived from the preselected name borane) (not boric triamide) 1,1′,1′′-boranetriyltrihydrazine (name derived from the preselected name borane) (not boric trihydrazide) Si(NH2)4 silanetetramine (name derived from the preselected name silane) (not silicic tetramide) Si(NH-NH2)4 1,1′,1′′,1′′′-silanetetrayltetrahydrazine (name derived from the preselected name silane ) (not silicic tetrahydrazi

records.title: Amides and hydrazides of nitric and nitrous acids
records.source_quote: Nitramines are amides of nitric acid (see ref.

records.title: Salts, esters, and anhydrides of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: The methodology discussed in this section is applicable to all mononuclear oxo acids whether or not they have retained names or names using infixes or prefixes.

records.title: Salts of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: Neutral salts of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids are named by citing the cation(s) followed by the name(s) of the anion(s) as a separate word.

records.title: Esters of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: Esters of mononuclear noncarbon acids are named in the same way as esters of organic acids (see P-65.6.3.2).

records.title: Anhydrides of mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids
records.source_quote: Neutral anhydrides formed between acids named by suffixes and mononuclear noncarbon oxoacids described in P-67.1.1 are named in the same way as described for anhydrides derived from carboxylic and the sulfur acids named by suffixes (see P-65.7).
